responseSet.title: Jones On Ice: Psychoanalysis And Figure Skating
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Jones On Ice: Psychoanalysis And Figure Skating

Todd Dufresne and Gary Genosko

Gary Genosko

Todd Dufresne
Gary Genosko
Programme in Social and Political Thought
York Univeristy
North York
Ontario M3J 1P3

Gary Genosko  

This paper rescues Ernest Jones's largely-unknown book, ‘The Elements of Figure Skating’ (1931/1952), from relative obscurity and argues for its place in the psychoanalytic literature. In the first section, the authors situate Jones's passion for figure skating by turning to various historical and biographical sources, and to personal correspondence with Jones's son, Mervyn Jones. In the second section, they offer the first textual reading of Jones and skating within a psychoanalytic framework. This approach, they argue, is long overdue since Jones explicitly claimed that the ‘Elements’ was motivated by his interest in the ‘psychological’ problems of the beginning skater, and with the task of instruction therein. The authors rely heavily on passages from the ‘Elements’, in part because the book is out-of-print and unavailable to most readers, but also because Jones's own words are an exemplary instance of his intense transference to psychoanalytic theory during his extra-professional time as a skater. They conclude the paper by arguing that Jones ultimately advocates a position beyond psychology when he idealistically embraces ‘exact knowledge’ as the royal road to masterful skating.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Entrevista y Diagnóstico en Psiquiatría y Psicología Infantil Psicoanalítica.

By Eulàlia Torras de Beà. Barcelona; Buenos Aires and Mexico: Paidós. 1991, Pp 207.

Raquel Zac De Goldstein

Raquel Zac De Goldstein

Raquel Zac De Goldstein
M. Ram
ó
n Castilla 2943, 1425 Buenos Aires

Raquel Zac De Goldstein  

This attractive book by Eulàlia Torras de Beà, whose title means ‘Interview and diagnosis in psychiatry and psychoanalytic child psychology’, is a training publication solidly grounded in clinical practice. It comprises essentially a clinical study of the interview with children and adolescents, using an unequivocally psychoanalytic approach based on the thought and concepts of Freud, Klein and her followers, and a number of other important psychoanalytic thinkers. A detailed, readable and vivid account is given of a series of interviews as conducted and subsequently discussed in study groups specifically formed for the purpose. It would, in my opinion, be unfair to the author to attempt a summary of this wealth of clinical material, which, aptly presented and interpreted, demonstrates the importance and decisive value the author attributes to the interview as a guide to the (presumably psychiatric and/or psychological) treatment of mental disorders.

Let us simply mention the depth of observation and reflection revealed by some of the interviews which interested us most, for example the one entitled ‘Manuel, the closed window’, which illustrates and clearly examines regressive and phobic object relations vis-à-vis the new and the changes in the interaction between the parents and the child.

We would also single out the subtle interactions revealed by the three instructive interviews of ‘Mireia, dancing off the floor’, which allow us to glimpse something more of what the author defines as the functions of a diagnostic interview.

Chapter VI, ‘Rivers of blood’, which presents an interview with two children and their mother, shows us the study group at work, with its counter-transferences and identifications as they emerge in the various situations studied. There follow ‘Tristan, filling in his form’, ‘A mother, a girl’ (two interviews a week apart), ‘Lidia, at school’ and, finally, ‘Norma, that will not do’.

As already stated, much of the rich content of these clinical examples would be lost in any attempt to summarise them. Instead, we shall therefore comment on Torras de Beà’s reflections on the interviews.

responseSet.title: The Mutual Search For Self In The Analytic Dyad
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Mutual Search For Self In The Analytic Dyad

Julia Fabricius

Julia Fabricius

Julia Fabricius
60 Bloomsbury Street
London WC1B 3QT

Julia Fabricius  

Clinical material is presented from seven consecutive sessions in the third year of an analysis to show the way in which patient and analyst worked at the task of allowing each of their separate self and object representations to emerge. These emergent representations are ones in which different aspects of the self, both ‘good’ and ‘bad’, are gradually integrated. The continued tension in both participants between an infantile narcissistic view of self and object and a realistic integrated one is highlighted. The way in which the analyst's psychic work on the acceptance of her own narcissism aids the patient's integration of disowned aspects of self is discussed. This material is presented within the framework of ideas about the development of a theory of mind and of reflective self function proposed by Fonagy et al. (1993a, b) and in the context of recent writing on the analyst's unconscious participation in the analytic process, e.g. Levine (1994).

responseSet.title: Obituary
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Obituary

Robert Wallerstein

Robert Wallerstein

Robert S. Wallerstein
290 Beach Road
Belvedere
CA 94920

Robert Wallerstein  

MERTON MAX GILL (1914-1994)

Merton Gill occupied a unique place in my intellectual and emotional scheme of things. As close friend and colleague, as scientific ideal and moral gadfly, and as research mentor and supporter, albeit—despite mutual wishes that would have had it otherwise—we never worked collaboratively in the same setting. I first met Merton in the early ’50s—now more than four decades ago—when I, at the very start of my career as a psychotherapy researcher was fashioning, together with some colleagues, what evolved into the very massive and ambitious 30-year-long Psychotherapy Research Project of The Menninger Foundation, and was applying to the Foundations’ Fund for Research in Psychiatry for my first extramural grant funding. Merton, whom I knew then only through his early writings on psychotherapy and psychoanalysis, which had indeed played a signal role in the conceptual organisation of our project, came as one of the site visitors. He was then, at our first meeting, his characteristic incisive, lucid, probing, at times acerbic and uncomfortably probing, but always at the same time, curiously respectful, self. After the visit none of us could be sure of Merton’s intellectual take on the project but in due course we were informed that we were awarded the grant which helped launch our project on its long career.

It was this show of confidence by Merton in what I could produce that continued to mark and helped to sustain my whole future research career, as when years later, in 1966, I left Topeka and my active directorship of the still ongoing research programme, in order to take up my continuing career in San Francisco, and yet somehow still carry out my commitment to the final clinical accounting of the work and the results of this psychotherapy research project. That is, my book, 42 Lives in Treatment, the chronicle of this 30-year-long research programme, was at that time only a dream for the future, and it was Merton’s continuing faith that I would not shirk or disappoint this task that not only helped enable me to accomplish it, but required that I do so. Incidentally, it was also Merton who suggested the title of the book, but the account of that amusing vignette would be too long a digression here.

responseSet.title: In Vienna Veritas
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

In Vienna Veritas

James Naiman

James Naiman

James Naiman
2156 Sherbrooke West, Suite 6, Montreal,
PQ H3H 1G7

James Naiman  

Dear Mr Tuckett,

In his paper ‘In Vienna Veritas … ?’ (IJPA 75, 511-583, 1994), Riccardo Steiner quoted (p. 541) Freud’s comment about infantile wishes on p. 553 of The Interpretation of Dreams: ‘If I may use a simile, they are only capable of annihilation in the same sense as the ghosts in the underworld of the Odyssey—ghosts which awoke to new life as soon as they tasted blood’.

Since there is no mention of tasting blood in the Odyssey (lines 35-7 of Book XI read:‘... I [Odysseus] took the sheep and cut their throats over the pit, and the dark blood ran forth. Then these gathered from Erebus the spirits of those that are dead ...’), I thought perhaps Freud had incorporated another Greek text into his simile. A passage in Oedipus at Colonus seemed a possible candidate; lines 621-2 read: ‘when my slumbering and buried corpse, cold in death, shall one day drink their warm blood’. The speaker is Oedipus and ‘their’ refers to his sons.

While there is no mention of Oedipus at Colonus in The Standard Edition, Peter Gay has written: ‘In late life Freud liked to call his daughter, Anna, his Antigone … It is Antigone who, in Oedipus at Colonus, leads her blind father by the hand—’(1988, p. 92)

Freud recognised his filicidal wishes when he wrote: ‘the concealed impulse … which might have found satisfaction in the dreaded accident to my son; it is the envy which is felt for the young by those that have grown old’ (The Interpretation of Dreams, p. 560).

Strachey said that these lines were added as a footnote in 1919 and incorporated into the text in 1930. If my hypothesis is correct, Freud’s simile would reveal filicidal wishes as well as a wish for immortality. These matters have been discussed at some length in my paper ‘Freud, Odysseus and Middle Age’, which was published in the Canadian Journal of Psychoanalysis, 2, 19-27, 1994.

Reference

Gay, P. (1988). Freud, a Life for Our Time. London & Melbourne: Dent.

Sincerely yours

403

responseSet.title: Letter
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Letter

Peter Hoffer

Peter Hoffer

Peter T. Hoffer
151 Bishop Ave, #L-14
Secane, PA 19018

Peter Hoffer  

Dear Sir:

I would like to provide some additional factual information which may help to illuminate certain points in Harold Blum’s article ‘The confusion of tongues and psychic trauma’, published in The International Journal of Psycho-Analysis (1994, p. 871).

A major aspect of Dr Blum’s thesis is his assertion that Ferenczi’s fatal illness, pernicious anaemia, had a significant impact on the writing of his controversial ‘Confusion of tongues’ paper. While it is difficult to date the diagnosis of his progressive illness with precision, and while its symptoms likely appeared some time before the paper was written, it should be pointed out that, from Ferenczi’s perspective, the acute stages of the illness did not occur until after he delivered the paper at the International Psychoanalytic Congress in Wiesbaden in September 1932. In a letter to Georg Groddeck of 20 March 1933, Ferenczi wrote (Ferenczi & Groddeck, 1986, p. 87):

My indisposition [Unwohlsein] in Baden-Baden was the beginning of a very dangerous anemia, which almost overpowered me in France, so that I was able to drag myself home only with difficulty and earlier than I had intended. The psychic cause of the decline was, in addition to exhaustion, the well-known disappointment with Freud (my italics; my translation).

It should also be noted that, although the issues of psychoanalytic technique which made the paper so controversial had already been occupying him for several years, Ferenczi did not begin writing it until after mid-June of 1932 (unpublished letter of Ferenczi to Freud, 11 June 1932). That Ferenczi likely did not know he was dying until after the paper was written, coupled with his long history of hypochondriasis, which, as we know from his correspondence with Freud, can be traced back at least as far as 1912 (letters 324 and 330 in Brabant et al., pp. 407 and 414) suggests that one should perhaps place a lesser emphasis than Dr Blum does on the extent to which Ferenczi’s organic illness contributed to the motivational content of the paper.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Wanderungen Zwischen Kontinenten. Gesammelte Schriften zur Psychoanalyse. [Journeys Between Continents. Collected Works on Psychoanalysis.]

By Erich Simenauer. Edited with a bibliographical postscript by Ludger M. Hermanns. With an introduction by Hermann Beland. 2 volumes. Stuttgart: Frommann-Holzboog. 1993. Pp. 667.

Caroline Neubahr

Caroline Neubahr

Caroline Neubahr
Habelschwerdter Allee 16
D 14195 Berlin

Caroline Neubahr  

Erich Simenauer was born in 1901 in Gleiwitz, Silesia, and was buried in 1988 in the Jewish Cemetery in Berlin. Thus he belongs by origin to what Eliade called ‘those’ times, that mythical pre-war period of persecution and emigration. These Journeys between Continents (the excellent title under which his works have been collected) and psychoanalysis together formed his destiny. The editor, Hermanns—the spiritus historicus of the Berlin Institute of Psychoanalysis—plausibly associates his love of history with ‘ancestor worship’. In this spirit, and in the awareness that reverence of this kind is the precondition of continuity and future itself, he has produced an exemplary edition. He has shared this Hesiodian task with Beland, whereby Hermanns deals with ‘the life’ of Simenauer, and Beland ‘the works’. When Simenauer describes paintings, for instance Savinio’s Annunciation or H. Henry Gowa’s La Retraite, colour reproductions naturally are provided. Journeys between Continents

responseSet.title: Three Instances of Injustice.: By K. R. Eissler. Madison, CT: International Universities Press, Inc. 1994. Pp. 265.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Three Instances of Injustice.

By K. R. Eissler. Madison, CT: International Universities Press, Inc. 1994. Pp. 265.

Lorraine Siggins

Lorraine Siggins

Lorraine D. Siggins
Yale University Health Services
17 Hillhouse Avenue, New Haven, Ct 06520-8237

Lorraine Siggins  

Three Instances of Injustice is a very personal book by an eminent psychoanalyst. It contains three unrelated essays that are loosely tied together under the rubric of ‘Injustice’. They touch on subjects such as childhood sexual assault, child custody and parental visitation, the Supreme Court, abortion, capital punishment, memories of Freud's relationship to his sister-in-law, the relationship between Freud and Jung and aspects of psychoanalytic technique when patients hear information about the analyst's reputation. Dr Eissler states in the introduction that he addresses the issues with ‘a nuance more passion and vehemence then is usual’, and thus the essays have the quality of a personal manifesto.

The first essay is entitled ‘Dr Elizabeth Morgan's contempt of court, or justified suspicion and conclusive evidence of sexual abuse of children’. The situation was as follows. Dr Morgan was jailed for having sent her daughter into hiding in order to prevent unsupervised visits from her father, Dr Foretich. Morgan felt that her former husband had sexually abused their daughter and sought to have the father's visits supervised. When the judge decided that the visits with Foretich could be unsupervised, Morgan hid her daughter and refused to reveal her whereabouts. The judge then sent Morgan to jail, charging her with contempt of court. There she remained for about two years. In discussing this case, Eissler takes Morgan's side and presents his views on the subject with vehemence indeed. Eissler considers it a great injustice that Morgan was incarcerated for this length of time for doing what any concerned mother would do if she believed, as Morgan did, that the child's father was sexually abusing her.

Eissler uses magazine accounts to portray pictures of Morgan and Foretich and the judge involved. He is quite scathing about the judge and the judicial system. He also takes this case as an opportunity to discuss a number of subjects about which he feels strongly. For instance, in describing a psychiatrist who appeared as a witness for Foretich, Eissler states, ‘Her distortion of facts is particularly revolting since it was done to the detriment of a woman smarting under a catastrophic upheaval of her existence’. Eissler frequently uses words like revolting to emphasise his feelings in the situation. Eissler also, in this same essay, makes excursions into discussing the Supreme Court, Judge Rehnquist, abortion and capital punishment. His comments are more often an expression of outrage than reasoned argument. In his discussion of the Supreme Court, Eissler is very critical and clearly has a particular political viewpoint which he considers the only ‘just’ one. For instance, in talking about President Reagan's appointments to the Supreme Court, he states: ‘Reagan, who is the personification of America's evil spirit, did his best not only to deprive the U. S. of its solid foundation, but also to destroy the country's liberalism, present and future’ (p. 68).

responseSet.title: Constitutive Signifiers Or Fetishes In Shakespeare's The Merchant Of Venice?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Constitutive Signifiers Or Fetishes In Shakespeare's The Merchant Of Venice?

B. Sokol

B. Sokol

B. J. Sokol
University of London
Goldsmith
’
s College
London SE14 6NW

B. Sokol  

Assessments of psychodynamic structures and processes implicit in ‘The Merchant of Venice’ vary considerably according to whether Lacanian or object-relations tenets are applied. Aspects of the play resemble a pattern described in Lacan's seminar on E. A. Poe's story ‘The purloined letter’: the ‘itinerary’ of the ‘signifier’ represented by a roving letter in Poe's story closely matches in outline the adventures surrounding roving rings in Shakespeare's play. Using Poe's story as evidence, Lacan's seminar argues that human identity is a product of ‘signifiers’, and not vice-versa. This conclusion that human subjectivities are constituted by signifiers does not stand up well in relation to the power of the rings and other signifying physical objects appearing in Shakespeare's play and its source stories. Recently expanded understandings of Freud's ideas about fetishism better explain the often malevolent power of the play's many signifying physical objects. In these the essential potential for damage is to sound personal relations with inner and outer objects of love. Explanations emphasising that distorted object relations and damaged subjectivities may be redeemed accord well with the dramatic and poetic patterns of Shakespeare's play, indicating that it, while portraying painful dilemmas, is still a comedy.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Wilfred Bion: His Life and Work, 1897—1979.

By Gérard Bléandonu, translated by Claire Pajaczkowska; foreword by R.D. Hinshelwood. New York: The Guilford Press. 1994. pp 303 + xii.

Aaron Esman

Aaron Esman

Aaron H. Esman
Payne-Whitney Clinic, NY Hospital
—
Cornell Medical Center
525 East 68th Street, New York, NY 10021

Aaron Esman  

As psychoanalysis approaches its centenary, appraisal and reappraisal of members of its pantheon has become a flourishing industry. A notable beneficiary of this tendency has been Sandor Ferenczi, lately rehabilitated and elevated to the status of a visionary figure by recent biographers who have, persuasively, claimed for him a major role in presaging the evolving shift towards object-relational perspectives and towards technical flexibility.

Now it is the turn of Wilfred Bion to be the subject of a biographical study which seeks to explicate and define his contributions to some of the newer directions in psychoanalysis. Like some of the principal advocates for Ferenczi, the present spokesman for Bion is French, described as a ‘community psychiatrist’ who, though apparently not officially a psychoanalyst, is thoroughly grounded in psychoanalytic theory and obviously dedicated to its practice. Recognising the complexity, even the maddening difficulty of Bion's writings, he struggles to clarify the master's thought and to place it in the psychoanalytic and philosophical traditions that, he believes, both nourished and benefited from it.

Bléandonu succeeds in organising Bion's work into four more or less chronological segments: his pre-analytic studies of group processes (still probably his most influential work); his Kleinian-influenced efforts to understand and treat the psychotic patient and the ‘psychotic part’ of the neurotic personality; his tortured efforts to formulate a ‘scientific’ psychoanalytic epistemology; and his late ventures into autobiography and ‘science fiction’. Further, he convincingly relates aspects of Bion's intellectual development to his experience as an Anglo-Indian child, displaced and separated in his latency years to an upper-crust English boarding school in which he was treated as an outsider and subjected to the hazing and homosexual abuses characteristic of such institutions, at least at that time. It was the last of these experiences that generated in him, as he said, ‘an absolute hatred and loathing of sexuality in any form’, which seems both to have delayed his entry into marriage until the age of 45 and to have shaped, in some degree, the propensity for abstract intellectualisation that pervades his theoretical efforts.

[[RunningHead]]

Obituary

Aaron Coleman

Aaron Coleman

Aaron Coleman
49 West 12th Street
New York
New York 10011

Aaron Coleman  

DONALD M. KAPLAN (1927-1994)

On 20 September 1994, Dr Donald Kaplan died at the age of 67, several days after surgery for a dissecting aortic aneurysm. His death was sudden, unexpected; and for his family, friends, colleagues and students it was hard to believe that a mind so keen and a voice so distinct could be stilled. His individual mark was on everything he did. In his public presentations, in his papers, in his private conversations there were echoes of the Bronx, extravagant epigrammatic elegance, jokes that were sometimes mischievous and sometimes gave resonant illumination to his ideas and formulations, but at the core a seriousness of discourse that came from his long and intense devotion to the psychoanalytic precincts of the life of the mind. It was the address where he most truly resided.

He had first hoped to become a writer and, after his graduation from City College and his marriage to Louise Miller, they lived for the better part of a year in Majorca with Donald working on a novel and Louise painting. It was a pleasure and interest he never abandoned. Many happy hours over the years were devoted to his journals, correspondence and poetry. A book he wrote after the Cuban missile crisis with Armand Schwerner and Louise, The Domesday Dictionary (1963) is an impossible-to-describe, analytically-informed ironic inventory of the cultural detritus tumbling from a civilisation playing with nuclear annihilation. In recent years, he was still collecting material for an offbeat screenplay about complications following a caper using silk stockings to extract gold from the ocean bed off Coney Island.

After returning to this country, he had a brief flirtation with the ideas of Wilhelm Reich, but his intellectual passions increasingly centred around Freud and psychoanalysis, and he simultaneously pursued his doctorate in clinical psychology at NYU and his psychoanalytic training at the National Association for the Advancement of Psychoanalysis under Theodore Reik, completing both in the early 60s. During the years that followed, his prodigious creative output included more than 40 papers and 80 book reviews and popular articles, in publications including The Tulane Drama Review, The Village Voice, Harpers, as well as The Psychoanalytic Review, The Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association, The International Journal of Psycho-Analysis and The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child. He was able to write for an educated audience beyond the professional journals without compromising the rigour of his thinking or diminishing the complexity of psychoanalytic theory or practice. His capacity to compress high seriousness and puckish humour within an elegant prose style served him well. Everywhere in Donald's papers one also finds a turn of phrase, a way of perceiving human experience that enlivens and deepens usual psychological constructs.

[[RunningHead]]

The Rhetorical Voice of Psychoanalysis: Displacement of Evidence by Theory.

By Donald P. Spence. Cambridge: Harvard University Press. 1994. Pp. 228.

Joseph Caston

Joseph Caston

Joseph Caston
2710 Broderick Street
San Francisco, CA 94123

Joseph Caston  

With The Rhetorical Voice of Psychoanalysis, Spence offers his third book-length argument on the problems of clinical theory. In each of these works, he puts psychoanalysis through a gutting criticism and dissection, drawing it to the precipice. Were any potential anti-Freudian to consider but a few of the problems thus targeted, he would waste no time with psychoanalysis, and kick the groaning corpus, whole, over the edge. Spence, however, commits himself each time to rescue and repair.

In Narrative Truth and Historical Truth

Copyright © Institute of Psycho-Analysis, London, 1995

[[RunningHead]]

Reply to Drs Emanuel Berman and Peter Hoffer

Harold Blum

Harold Blum

Harold P. Blum
23 The Hemlocks
Roslyn Estates
NY 11576

Harold Blum  

I found Dr Emanuel Berman’s letter to be a stimulating, thoughtful, complementary, and challenging response to my paper. I agree with Dr Berman that it was most regrettable that Ernest Jones decided against publishing the ‘Confusion of tongues’ paper of Sándor Ferenczi in The International Journal of Psycho-Analysis. My paper addressed issues of silence and censorship, issues which deserved special consideration on the special occasion of the 75th Anniversary of The International Journal. The ‘conspiracy of silence’ which enveloped the ‘Confusion of tongues’ only amplified confusion instead of facilitating the clarification of very important psychoanalytic ideas and controversies. The ‘Confusion of tongues’ was also a metaphor and organising theme to depict and discuss some of the fascinating contributions and controversies engendered by Ferenczi’s paper, which remain of great contemporary relevance.

Dr Berman develops a line of thought demonstrating that Ferenczi’s interest in mutual analysis had its origins in his first years as an analyst and that this could be seen in his writings and correspondence. I am in accord with this point of view and repeatedly emphasised the regressive reactivation of earlier conflicts and traumata during the periods of Ferenczi’s illness and the writing of ‘Confusion of tongues’ and the ‘Clinical Diary’. Dr Berman seems to question the thesis of regressive proclivity while indirectly supporting it and extending the historical dimension with his illustrative data and discussion.

1047

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

In Memoriam: Commemorative Address For Wolfgang Loch, 1915-1995

Carl Nedelmann

Carl Nedelmann

Carl Nedelmann
Michael Balint Institut
Finkenau 19
D-22081 Hamburg

Carl Nedelmann  

In response to your enquiry, esteemed Herr Cotta, as to whether a monument … should be erected to the memory of our Schiller, I can for the present say no more than this … [:] I am convinced that art, should it ally itself with pain, ought to excite the latter only in order to mitigate it and resolve it into higher consoling feelings; and with this in mind I shall seek to represent not so much what we have lost as what remains to us (Goethe to Cotta, 1 June 1805).

I am to speak here of my brother, whom … my eyes no longer behold, but who is yet beside me at night in my dreams, when I have no notion of his decease. So let us keep him in our minds by a bundle of memories, which, however, as may be expected in this company, are confined almost exclusively to his scientific activity (Jacob Grimm's commemorative address for his brother Wilhelm at the Royal Academy of Sciences in Berlin on 5 July 1860).

Wolfgang Loch was born in Berlin on 10 May 1915 and died in Rottweil on 7 February 1995. My own acquaintance with him was as a student attending his lectures, as a psychoanalytic candidate in supervision, as senior physician in the department he headed, and as a colleague and friend who never on that account ceased to be his pupil.

He possessed wit and humour, a deep sympathy with his fellow-men and immeasurable intelligence. He had a fondness for eccentric positions. The most complicated subjects were grist to his mill and he expected his readers to grapple with them. Yet in his day-to-day clinical work he was renowned for the utter simplicity of his statements. He was a man of theory and of practice, a benevolent doctor and academic teacher, who established a chair of psychoanalysis at the University of Tübingen. He made substantial contributions to the development of the Balint Group method (Loch, 1995a). A founder member of the Stuttgart-Tübingen Psychoanalytic Study Group, he was its first President. For more than twenty years he was one of the editors of the Jahrbuch der Psychoanalyse. Of particular relevance to this occasion, he exercised a formative influence on the German Psychoanalytical Association (DPV) throughout his membership of that organisation, in which he held the office of President from 1972 to 1975, becoming member emeritus in 1990; he was constantly concerned to enrich the cause of psychoanalysis and to maintain strong and fruitful links with the International Psychoanalytical Association, on whose Executive Council he was a Vice-President and Secretary for Europe.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Self-Analysis: Critical Inquiries, Personal Visions.

Edited by James W. Barron. Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press. 1993. Pp. 328.

Kenneth Calder

Kenneth Calder

Kenneth Calder
110 East 78th Street, New York, NY 10021

Kenneth Calder  

This is an excellent book. It summarises the current thinking about self-analysis in a way that is interesting and comprehensive and opens doors for the future development of self-analysis.

Among the virtues of the book are the extent of its comprehension and the care of its integration. In many ways I learned from it. And I enjoyed it.

Along the way, the book addresses the following topics: Can self-analysis be done? If it can be done, what method or methods work best? Is there reason to favour spontaneous goals over systematic goals? or the reverse? And should any method of research be favoured?

It helps that the goals of self-analysis are appreciated by a careful editor (James W. Barron) and by more than a dozen thoughtful and imaginative contributors. I regret that not all the contributors are recognised in the brief descriptions that follow.

The Bernadis (of Uruguay) describe the wide-spread importance of ‘assumptions’ that influence the conduct of life, including the life of a psychoanalyst. Using quotes, they described the importance in any analysis of the wish to be analysed, and they see self-analysis as being ‘favourable to processes of change and revision’.

McLaughlin states that he has learned from patients who ‘stretch and burden us’. He lists stimuli that push him into periods of self-analysis. They include sleepiness, boredom, excessive intervening or (from Poland) ‘heightened psychic tension’. On the other hand ‘transference sanctuaries’, where one can ‘free float’, encourage self-analysis.

Smith employs the two-way exchange between analyst and patient whereby each learns from the other. He agrees with Gedo and Schwaber about the importance of the analysis of the patient’s perception of the analyst.

Wolf differs from several contributors in seeing transference as largely demands from the patient. He and Gedo are prompted more from their patients than from themselves. Gedo notes that it takes powerful forces in self-analysis to overcome strong resistances.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Obituary: Lionel Ovesey 1915-1995

Ethel Person

Ethel Person

Ethel Spector Person
135 Central Park West
New York City
NY 10023

Ethel Person  

Lionel Ovesey, a psychoanalytic pioneer in the study of homosexuality, sex, and gender, died on 21 May 1995, at the age of 80, after a series of debilitating strokes. His work is an integral part of the intellectual heritage of the Columbia University Center for Training and Research, founded in 1945. Ovesey—Lion as he was called—and Aaron Karush, two of the institute’s first graduates, along with its founders, Sandor Rado, George Daniels, Abram Kardiner, and David Levy, shaped the Center’s direction with their collective vision. Their thrust—groundbreaking in the 40s and 50s—was to broaden the basis of understanding psychological motivation, stressing the importance of adaptation and the need for establishing safety. They studied the impact of the cultural milieu on the psyche and they were intent on strengthening the scientific underpinnings of psychoanalysis.

Lion was born in Manchuria, where his Ukrainian parents had emigrated, and as a young boy the family moved to the United States. The contrast between the family’s lavish lifestyle in Manchuria and their relatively reduced circumstances in Los Angeles gave him an early and personal understanding of how different circumstances and cultures impact on one’s life. Central to his whole personality was the fusion of his sensitivity to (and identification with) the disadvantaged and his aristocratic personal style.

Lion graduated from the University of California in 1937, from the University of California Medical School in 1941, and interned at the Los Angeles County General Hospital. His medical education was interrupted by the war, and he spent four years in the United States Army, rising to the rank of Captain. As flight surgeon, he was exposed to the traumatic aftermath suffered by crash survivors and he became increasingly interested in psychological problems. He moved to New York City in 1945 in order to begin psychiatric training at Manhattan State Hospital and psychoanalytic training at Columbia. Lion had chosen to train at Columbia specifically because of its affiliation to a medical centre and the potential that affiliation offered for investigative research. Upon graduation, he was invited to join the faculty and soon became a Training and Supervising Analyst, as well as a Clinical Professor of Psychiatry at the Columbia University College of Physicians and Surgeons.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychotic Metaphysics

By Eric Rhode. London: The Clunie Press (Karnac Books). 1994. Pp 326 + xiv.

Paul Williams

Paul Williams

Paul Williams
1/15 Rosslyn Hill, London NW3 5UJ

Paul Williams  

This complex, creative, occasionally prolix but never dull book has been written in the form of a discursive meditation on the clinical, cultural and mythological significance of change as an experience of catastrophe. The author is a Kleinian psychoanalytical psychotherapist whose work is clearly influenced by Bion and Meltzer. He draws upon these writers and anthropological accounts of social change, including the work of van Gennep on rites de passage, to illustrate how, in a state of transition from paranoid-schizoid to depressive position functioning, mind is required to confront its psychotic robbing of life from ‘good objects’. Anton Obholzer, in his commentary on the book, describes the necessary consequence of this developmental theft as an ensuing ‘exploration of a geography of binary division, an underworld to which it [mind] has banished its counterpart, a twin foetus and its mother, whom it must come to acknowledge as the rightful inheritors of life’.

The catastrophic nature of change has occupied the thinking of a number of psychoanalysts, of whom Bion is perhaps the most influential. Donald Meltzer, introducing Rhode's book, notes how bouts of turbulence in ourselves and others are visible products of personal development.

[[RunningHead]]

Hans Keller: Essays on Music.

Edited by Christopher Wintle. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press. 1994. Pp. 269.

Michael Parsons

Michael Parsons

Michael Parsons
54 Primrose Gardens, London NW3 4TP

Michael Parsons  

‘As soon as I detest something I ask myself why I like it’: the words of someone with a thoroughly psychoanalytic curiosity about, and suspicion of, his own mental processes. J.C. Flugel wrote to him ‘Incidentally, if you are not a member of the British Psychoanalytic Society, I should be happy to propose you’. Psychoanalytic, then, but evidently not a psychoanalyst. And what was it that he so ambivalently detested? Answer: the Harry Lime theme.

Hans Keller is a name to conjure with in the world of music. He will be less known to psychoanalysts, although some may have come across his acerbic comments on the 1975 London IPA Congress in Music, Closed Societies and Football (Keller, 1986; London: Toccata). He was born in Vienna in 1919. He centred his life around music but he was always interested in psychoanalysis and convinced of its importance. He came to London as a refugee in 1938 and between then and his death in 1985 he became one of the leading figures in musical life in Britain. He was a writer and broad-caster and held various posts of importance at the BBC. He was a fine violinist and an expert on the string quartet (his book on the Haydn quartets is a classic). He coached professional string players and taught at the Menuhin School.

Keller's musical thinking was deeply informed by psychoanalysis, as this collection of his papers shows. The book, incidentally, is beautifully produced and a pleasure to handle. Christopher Wintle, another psychoanalytically minded musicologist, has selected some forty essays written between 1951 and 1984 and edited them with comprehensive notes and a biographical introduction. Early in his career Keller did write directly on psychological topics, publishing in the British Journal of Medical Psychology, but all the papers in this collection are about music. Keller's psychoanalytic interest is evident nonetheless, particularly in the first section on music criticism.

A good half of this fascinating book by the Swiss psychoanalyst Danielle Quinodoz consists of a systematic phenomenological account of various forms of vertigo, stemming from different psychogenic causes and displaying a variety of dynamic connotations. The two classical Freudian theories of anxiety and the Kleinian object-relations model of child development, carefully reviewed in Chapter 11, provide a mostly non-dogmatic underpinning to the author's taxonomy. Yet if offering such a classification, however sophisticated, were the main purpose of the book, it would be legitimate to question whether a relatively minor psychological symptom such as vertigo deserves the central stage Quinodoz appears to allocate to it. But because her aim is more ambitious, and her writing so creative, the concept of vertigo (and the French vertige sounds, to my ears at least, more suggestive than the Anglo-Latin term) becomes a sort of trampoline for her to perform some remarkable psychoanalytic somersaults.

After distinguishing vertigo of physical origins (a state of imbalance resulting from uncoordinated information provided by different sensory systems)

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Patterns in the Work of Graham Greene.

By Roland A. Pierloot. Amsterdam and Atlanta, GA: Rodopi. 1994. Pp. 264.

Emanuel Berman

Emanuel Berman

Emanuel Berman
Department of Psychology
University of Haifa, Haifa 31905, Israel

Emanuel Berman  

he tends to see himself as one of his characters, and the outlines of the characters in his novels seem to be studiedly imprecise … Greene at all events remains obscure in his life, his work, his person (Allain, 1983, p. 15).

Roland Pierloot, a senior Belgian psychoanalyst, presents here the fruits of his lengthy and loving involvement with the fiction of Graham Greene.

The introduction demonstrates Pierloot's serious and up-to-date attention to developments in the psychoanalytic study of literature. Quoting Felman, Wright, Brooks, Skura, and many other recent and earlier contributors, Pierloot discusses the pendulum swing from the traditional emphasis on ‘applying’ psychoanalytic insights to the study of the unconscious dynamics of authors or of literary figures to the more recent emphases on the text itself, on linguistic structures, and on the correspondence of literary and psychic processes. ‘Still, in our study we return to the old belief that the writings of an author also bear the stamp of his individual mental organization’, he states (p. 5). He rejects the psychiatric pathographic approach, expresses concerns about the risk of mixing divergent levels of data in attempted psychobiographies, and wisely reminds us that autobiographies can also be seen as ‘self inventions’, as novels in which the hero-narrator is a fictional being, an observation that is certainly relevant to Greene's autobiographical works.

Pierloot's stated preference is to focus on ‘the unconscious phantasies of the author, metamorphosed in his imaginary world, expressed in his writings’ (p. 7). Modestly he says: ‘We refrained from presuming a speculative unifying and integrating unconscious structure and we limited our study to the specification of different patterns of imaginary representation’ (p. 8). He discusses various models of unconscious motivations to create a literary text, and concludes that such motivations must be established individually for a particular author. He is also interested in the affects and identifications aroused in the reader, and concludes: ‘A psychoanalytic interpretation of a literary text originates with an analysis of the interpreter's countertransference’ (p. 23).

Castelnuovo-Tedesco's stature as a clinician par excellence is exemplified in this volume of his collected papers. In his preface, the author makes it clear that his primary interest is clinical and he remains close to observable behaviour. Clinical theory rather than metapsychology is the substrate for his thinking. This volume of collected papers spanning four decades addresses different audiences at different levels of sophistication and is sometimes of uneven quality. Since the papers have been published as written and not updated, some may appear outmoded although one obtains the sense of the sweep of the author's ideas over the years.

[[RunningHead]]

Letter

Riccardo Steiner

Riccardo Steiner

Riccardo Steiner
12A Belsize Lane, London NW3

Riccardo Steiner  

Dear Mr Tuckett,

Thank you for letting me see Dr Naiman’s letter.

In my paper I was interested in pointing out the quality of the images that Freud had used to describe the unconscious wishes and phantasies and not in establishing whether or not it came from Homer’s Odyssey—yet I thank Dr Naiman for his point and information—and connections. I cannot comment on his other hypothesis. Unfortunately I did not know about his 1994 paper because we do not receive the Canadian Journal of Psychoanalysis and up to the present there is no actualised database which can inform the scholar of everything that is simultaneously published on a certain subject in our field. Furthermore, my manuscript was accepted in autumn 1993. Due to its length and all the editing problems this created it would have been impossible to ask for further bibliographic items or changes in my text at this final stage.

Yours sincerely

[[RunningHead]]

Reconstruction in Psychoanalysis: Childhood Revisited and Recreated.

By Harold P. Blum. Madison, CT: International Universities Press. 1994. Pp. 196 + x.

Paul Dewald

Paul Dewald

Paul A. Dewald
4524 Forest Park Road, St Louis, MO 63108

Paul Dewald  

During the academic years 1980-85 a research/study group met monthly under the auspices of the Psychoanalytic Research and Development Fund. The 17-member group, chaired by Blum, developed ‘an organised, systematic investigation of the theory, technique and concept of reconstruction’. Throughout the book there are significant attributions to the group and its opinions, but responsibility for this monograph is the work of Blum himself. There are eight chapters, three of which are analytic case reports meant to illustrate the role of reconstruction in the psychoanalytic process. In the first chapter Blum presents an extensive list of questions raised by the working group regarding the role of reconstruction in the process of psychoanalysis. These issues orient the reader to some of the discussion in the subsequent material of the book.

Blum develops the history of the concept of reconstruction, beginning with Freud's early theory of trauma as the causative agent in psychoneurosis, and emphasises the limitations of Freud's use of the archaeological metaphor. He follows the precepts on reconstruction established by Freud as they were subsequently elaborated chiefly by Kris, Greenacre, and Blum himself. He develops a distinction between construction and reconstruction, terms which others have used synonymously. For Blum the former is an hypothesis developing in the mind of the analyst as he/she observes and responds to the totality of the analysand's material and experience. Many constructions are silently entertained by the analyst until offered to the patient in the form of an intervention, at which time Blum views it as a reconstruction. (Moore & Fine, 1990, prefer the term ‘construction’ to describe the entire process, and at most use the two terms synonymously.)

For Blum a genetic interpretation involves a single or specific intepretive comment narrowly focused on a particular childhood memory, behaviour, wish, or thought process. By contrast, reconstruction involves genetic formulations of which the patient does not have an actual memory or detailed recall. They are based on data such as transference, dreams, screen memories and fragments of memory, which the analyst synthesises in an integrated form, which may combine clarifications achieved in several genetic interpretations.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Human Feelings; Explorations in Affect Development and Meaning.

Edited by Steven L. Ablon, Daniel Brown, Edward J. Khantzian and John E. Mack. Hillsdale, NJ and London: Analytic Press. 1993. Pp. 431.

Anna Ornstein

Anna Ornstein

Anna Ornstein
4177 Rose Hill Avenue, Cincinnati, OH 45229

Anna Ornstein  

Although the centrality of emotional experiences in psychoanalysis has always been recognised, few contributions were made to this topic in the early years of psychoanalysis. Over the last twenty years, however, affect has become a major area of exploration and it has gradually replaced Freud's drive discharge theory as a major factor motivating human behaviour. This book's examination of emotions from various perspectives, by authors from various disciplines, is a welcome contribution to this rapidly expanding area in developmental psychology and clinical psychoanalysis.

It is difficult to avoid repetition in the chapters of edited books, but this disadvantage of multiple authorship has been minimised in this volume because of the many years of collaboration between the authors in the Harvard Affect Study Group. However, the chapters vary greatly in quality of content and style. For example, the chapters containing clinical material do not sufficiently stress affect development and meaning, which are the book's major emphasis. This book suffers from the common problem in psychoanalytic publications: carefully enunciated theory has little to do with the conceptualisation of the clinical material. The chapter on ‘Affect and addictive suffering’ is an exception; Khantzian seamlessly integrates disturbances in affect regulation with the clinical manifestations of addiction.

Considering the great variety of perspectives from which emotions have been examined, the editors have done a remarkable job in organising the book into five sections: ‘Theoretical considerations’, ‘Affect and the life cycle’, ‘Trauma, addiction and psychosomatics’, ‘Transformation of affect’ and ‘New directions’. The exhaustive lists of references following each chapter add enormously to the value of the book.

The first chapter, by Daniel Brown, is the conceptual foundation of the book.

responseSet.title: Narcissism—A New Theory.: By Neville Symington. London: Karnac Books. 1993. Pp. xix + 137.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Narcissism—A New Theory.

By Neville Symington. London: Karnac Books. 1993. Pp. xix + 137.

Martin Kinston

Martin Kinston

Martin Kinston
52 Platts Lane, London NW3 7NT

Martin Kinston  

The question of narcissism continues to occupy a central place in psychoanalytic thinking and practice. In Ovid's telling of the myth, the trauma for Narcissus is his failure to see beyond what is in front of his eyes, namely his idealised self. The youth eventually dies, ecstatically trapped within his enclosed world of oneness. Symington's book was inspired by a determination to engage in depth with this important theme, and he leads us into an investigation that questions the nature of our identity and the possibility of genuinely relating to ourselves and others. His work is the result of a series of lectures given at the Sydney Institute for Psychoanalysis, and the author's writing therefore has a directness of expression which successfully engages the reader.

Narcissism is important to Symington for two reasons. He sees it as a condition that lies at the root of all mental psychopathology and as a problem that has to be faced in normal human development.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Posttraumatic Nightmares: Psychodynamic Explorations.

By Melvin R. Lansky with Carol R. Bley. Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press. 1995. Pp. 188.

Howard Levine

Howard Levine

Howard B. Levine
124 Dean Road, Brookline, MA 02146

Howard Levine  

This book challenges a long-neglected contradiction embedded in the psychoanalytic theory of dreams. On the one hand, Freud's (1900) original model held that the manifest content of the dream was a defensive screen for latent dream thoughts linked to conflicts surrounding unfulfilled, unconscious, infantile wishes. The press of these wishes for gratification and discharge was intensified by the events of the day (the day residue) and opposed by the defensive operations of the ego (the dream work). A conflict ensued, which Freud believed had the potential to disturb the dreamer's sleep, unless it was resolved by a form of compromise: forbidden wishes would be represented in the manifest content of the dream, but only if they were disguised by the mechanisms of the primary process—condensation, displacement and symbolisation. Thus, Freud proposed that the manifest dream was a defensive distortion of the ‘true’ (latent) dream thoughts that lay beneath it and that the dream was the guardian of sleep.

Twenty years later, however, in considering repetitive dreams that occurred following trauma, Freud (1920) concluded that their manifest contents were more or less accurate accounts of actual events, which were being dreamt in an attempt to master trauma. In this second dream theory, Freud excepted the post-traumatic dream from the rule that the instigation and raison d’être of the dream was the gratification of unconscious infantile wishes and the protection of the dreamer's need to sleep. He also conceptually freed the manifest content of such dreams from the assumption that it was a disguised, multiply-determined representation of unconscious, conflicted infantile wishes. Thus, Freud viewed the structure and function of the post-traumatic nightmare as being more like an affectively charged memory than a ‘true’ dream.

responseSet.title: Common Ground, Uncommon Methods
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Common Ground, Uncommon Methods

Cecilio Paniagua

Cecilio Paniagua

Cecilio Paniagua
Coraz
ó
n de Mar
í
a 2
28002 Madrid

Cecilio Paniagua  

The author, an American-trained psychoanalyst, currently a member of a theoretically heterogeneous European psychoanalytic society, reflects on his experiences with the different types of analysis practised in continental Europe and in the United States. Sharing some ‘common ground’ assumptions does not mean that analysts worldwide use comparable clinical methodology. Practitioners from disparate schools differ not only in their metatheoretical frameworks, but also in their theories of technique. Differences in clinical methods affect the scientific quality of clinical researches and, probably, influence therapeutic outcome. The lack of commonality in psychoanalytic methods often seems related to the disparate uses of logical fallacies in clinical reasoning; this, in turn, may be a consequence of socio-historical determinants. Several discussions of one clinical presentation are supplied as an illustration. It is suggested that efforts be made to further examine the relative validity of disparate inference-making models, and the practical results of applying different clinical methods.

responseSet.title: A Particular Perspective On Impasses In The Clinical Situation: Further Reflections On Psychoanalytic Listening
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Particular Perspective On Impasses In The Clinical Situation: Further Reflections On Psychoanalytic Listening

Evelyne Schwaber

Evelyne Schwaber

Evelyne Albrecht Schwaber
4 Welland Road
Brookline
MA 02146

Evelyne Schwaber  

We tend to think of impasses as manifest, if often puzzling events, but they may also occur quietly, disguised. The analytic process may seem to be moving on course—yet, on further glance, there can be subtle evidence that some impasse, at times just emerging, may be, perhaps collusively, evaded. Often outside the patient's awareness, cues to its occurrence may be expressed in the vicissitudes of affect or state or shift in content. When such phenomena go unnoted as vital communications, ultimately, a more dramatic eruption may take place; or, perhaps more insidiously, some central conflictual feature of the patient's character continues unexamined, unabated. The author suggests an alteration in how we think about impasses—how pervasive they may be, even as we may believe we are seeing the ordinary ebbs and flows of resistances and defensive processes. Drawing upon four clinical examples, the effort is made to elucidate the link between our understanding and recognition of the presence of an impasse and our mode of analytic listening.

[[RunningHead]]

Unimaginable Storms: A Search For Meaning in Psychosis.

By Murray Jackson and Paul Williams. Karnac Books. London. 1994. Pp. 248.

Richard Lucas

Richard Lucas

Richard Lucas
63 Ossulton Way, London N2 0JY

Richard Lucas  

Sir Dennis Hill, when Professor of Psychiatry at the Institute of Psychiatry and the Maudsley Hospital, provided his beds for the purpose of the application of psychoanalytic ideas in general psychiatry. John Steiner, who wrote the foreword of this book, was the first psychoanalytically-trained consultant to the Unit. When, in 1975, he moved to the Tavistock Clinic, Murray Jackson was appointed as his successor.

This book describes Murray Jackson's work on the unit over the 13-year period until his retirement in 1987, when the unit closed. The unit consisted of 11 beds on a 22-bed ward, shared with Professor Cawley, who was not an analyst but had an eclectic approach to general psychiatry.

The book starts with a background description of the two authors. Murray Jackson was born in Australia and came to train at the Maudsley 45 years ago. Initially he trained in analytical psychology, becoming familiar with Jung's work on psychosis. Subsequently he trained as a psychoanalyst, influenced by Klein, Rosenfeld, Segal, Bion and particularly Henri Rey, who was working in the Psychotherapy Department at the Maudsley. He worked part-time in the unit and part-time in analytic practice.

responseSet.title: Psychoanalytic-Marxism: Groundwork.: By Eugene Victor Wolfenstein. New York and London: The Guilford Press. 1993. Pp. 468.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic-Marxism: Groundwork.

By Eugene Victor Wolfenstein. New York and London: The Guilford Press. 1993. Pp. 468.

Donald Moss

Donald Moss

Donald Moss
80 University Place, 5th Floor, New York City
NY 10003

Donald Moss  

Eugene Wolfenstein’s Psychoanalytic-Marxism: Groundwork is a massive, thorough, impassioned work. Though ‘Marxism’ is the title’s noun, and ‘psychoanalytic’ its adjective, Wolfenstein even-

Copyright © Institute of Psycho-Analysis, London, 1995

208

handedly addresses both the powers and the limitations of each. His book is profoundly and ambitiously theoretical, but there is nothing airy or disengaged about it. Rather, its theoretical abstractions are consistently tethered to a ground which Wolfenstein quickly and effectively defines by way of a concrete and practical question: ‘What theory or theories facilitate the interpretation and transformation of situations characterized by human suffering?’ (p. 3).

This question, because Wolfenstein poses it so quickly and bluntly and proceeds to address it so systematically and seriously, might serve as a refreshing, if daunting, reminder to many psychoanalysts that our demanding practices have the unwanted side effect of walling us off from much of the human suffering which our clinicallygrounded theories might well have the power to address.

The word ‘reminder’ here refers to a second register of Wolfenstein’s text as well. He devotes two particularly illuminating chapters to the history of ‘Freudian-Marxism’, reminding us of their burgeoning conceptual and political interconnectedness, particularly between the two world wars.

In these chapters, he adroitly works his way through Reich, Fromm and Marcuse, and then moves on to the phenomenological turns of Sartre and Fanon. He summarises the work of Kovel and Lichtman, of Habermas and of the influence of the New Left, of feminist developments such as those of Gayle Rubin and Jessica Benjamin, and finally of post-structuralists such as Foucault. For each thinker, Wolfenstein seeks and finds his/her theory’s central contradictions and insufficiencies. Wolfenstein looks both within the theories and at their supporting social milieux. He makes a particularly persuasive argument that, just as in clinical practice, no emancipatory social theory can be truly effective, truly mutative, without being organically yoked to the appropriate emerging surface phenomena.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Correspondence of Sigmund Freud and Sándor Ferenczi.

Volume 1, 1908-1914. Edited by Eva Brabant, Ernst Falzeder, Patrizia Giampieri-Deutsch, under supervision of André Haynal. Transcribed by Ingeborg Meyer-Palmedo. Translated by Peter T. Hoffer. Introduction by André Haynal. Cambridge, Mass. and London: The Belknap Press of Harvard University Press. 1993. Pp. 584.

Judith Dupont

Judith Dupont

Judith Dupont
24 Place Dauphine, 75001 Paris

Judith Dupont  

This is surely the most important of all the correspondences bequeathed to us by Freud, by virtue both of its duration (twenty-five years) and of the exceptional quality of the relationship between the two correspondents. It covers every subject without exception. And, in the case of Ferenczi, without reserve too. As for Freud, reserve seems to have been a trait of his character: his whole nature bears its stamp, even where such a close friend as Ferenczi is concerned.

On 18 January 1908, a young Budapest neurologist, Sándor Ferenczi, wrote to Sigmund Freud, ‘whose teachings have occupied me incessantly for approximately a year’, requesting a meeting with him and his colleague, Dr Stein. Freud replied on 30 January, setting a time for the two Hungarian doctors to visit him on Sunday, 2 February. This was the beginning of a correspondence that continued for twenty-five years and comprised some 1250 communications, ranging from telegrams to letters of twelve pages or more.

Haynal describes in his introduction how the entire correspondence, consisting of both protagonists' letters, came into the hands of Balint. Having been charged by Gizella Ferenczi to publish it in its entirety, Balint was unable during his lifetime to secure the permissions necessary to bring this project to fruition. It was only after his death in the late 1980s, when none of the members of the two correspondents' immediate families was still alive, that full publication of the letters became possible. However, once all the obstacles had been eliminated, many preliminary operations remained to be carried out before work on the eventual edition could begin.

Balint had himself made a typewritten transcript of the letters, but this had to be carefully checked against the originals, in places corrected, and finally supplemented by a few subsequently discovered letters. Responsibility for this work was assumed by Meyer-Palmedo, who also contributed precious notes on the formal aspect of the letters wherever appropriate, as well as detailed comments to facilitate the placing of the small number of undated letters.

responseSet.title: A Triumph of the Eye over the Gaze? Everything You Always Wanted to Know about Lacan but were Afraid to Ask Hitchcock.: Edited by Slavoj Zizek. London: Verso Books. 1992. Pp. 279.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Triumph of the Eye over the Gaze? Everything You Always Wanted to Know about Lacan but were Afraid to Ask Hitchcock.

Edited by Slavoj Zizek. London: Verso Books. 1992. Pp. 279.

Thomas Elsaesser

Thomas Elsaesser

Thomas Elsaesser
Nieuwe Doelenstraat 16
1012 CP Amsterdam, The Netherlands

Thomas Elsaesser  

Early in 1992, when the term ‘ethnic cleansing’ entered the language and images of the civil war in former Yugoslavia were still blistering our eyes, a philosopher from the newly-independent Republic of Slovenia was invited to give a lecture at a prestigious American university. To the consternation of his hosts, he chose as his topic: ‘Why do the birds attack in Hitchcock's The Birds?’ At the end, someone from the audience burst out: ‘How can you talk about such a trifling subject when your ex-country is dying in flames?’ The philosopher, Slavoj Zizek, was quick with his answer, and before the year was out, he had published—in French, German and English—a collection of essays by himself and some of his colleagues in Lubljana, entitled Everything you always wanted to know about Lacan but were afraid to ask Hitchcock—a spoof of a spoof title, in that it is a parody of a Woody Allen parody of a sex education manual.

Uncannily prolific (an average of a book a year since 1989), ubiquitous at conferences and symposia in Paris and New York, London and Los Angeles, Zizek has re-injected an enormous energy and verve into a topic that most considered played-out and tired: the relevance of Lacan to either philosophy or psychoanalysis. After devastating accounts of the École Freudienne and damaging biographies of its founding father had made of Lacan just another fallen idol (along with Louis Althusser and Michel Foucault) in the pantheon of French intellectual gods, here was a sustained attempt to demonstrate that Lacan's famous seminars from the 1960s and 1970s (collected in Écrits) had a sub-text (Western philosophy from Plato to Kant and Hegel to Heidegger) and constituted an oblique but nonetheless perfectly legible commentary on the crisis of society now often referred to as ‘postmodernism’. It seems that it took someone from Eastern Europe, from the West's unconscious (barred, so to speak, until the fall of the Berlin Wall in 1989), to come up with the notion that an important aspect of capitalist culture and contemporary subjectivity had been prefigured all along in the unlikely constellation of Lacan and Hitchcock.

[[RunningHead]]

Body, Blood and Sexuality: A Psychoanalytic Study of St. Francis's Stigmata and their Historical Context.

By Nitza Yarom. Studies in History and Culture, Volume 4. Edited by Norman Cantor. New York: Peter Lang. 1992. Pp. 148.

Nina Coltart

Nina Coltart

Nina Coltart
1 Woodland Avenue,
Leighton Buzzard, Bedfordshire LU7 7JW

Nina Coltart  

An attractive feature of this unusual short book is that it takes hysteria seriously. It is generally assumed in psychiatry today that the prevalence of hysteria, which gave Freud so much of his material for creating the foundations of psychoanalysis, has greatly reduced during the twentieth century; the understanding of character disorder, with special reference to narcissism, has taken the dominant place in the literature. But whether hysteria has really declined is open to doubt. In fact, it is hysterical manifestations that have changed, owing to several major sociological trends, such as improvement in the roles and opportunities available to women. Hysteria has also suffered by entering the vernacular as a pejorative term; its loss as a rich and informative diagnosis has left psychiatry the poorer. Psychoanalysis, so often attacked from many angles today, can be said to have held the corner for hysteria, in spite of the reduction in its dramatic psychophysical symptoms such as conversion. This book, which skilfully weaves together a scholarly approach to mediaeval religious history with fluent handling of psychoanalytic theory, does much to redress the balance.

St. Francis of Assisi, who developed the stigmata in 1224, two years before his death, is the first authentically recorded case of this kind. Since then, there have been about three hundred, predominantly Catholic women. Sixty-one have been made saints, though none since the late eighteenth century. During the nineteenth century, the status of stigmatics went through a change from saint to patient, varying nevertheless according to the strength of Catholicism in the social context. Writers who have made a special study of the condition, either historically or medically, are about evenly divided between those inclining to a psychophysical explanation and those who lean more towards that of fraud or conscious self-induction. The author of this book remains throughout commendably free of the several biases open to her, and indicates early on that her detailed investigations convince her that in the case of St. Francis we are dealing with authentic hysteria, i.e. the unconscious production of a true conversion symptom. My own conviction would be based also on the fact (which she does not discuss)

[[RunningHead]]

The Spectrum of Psychoanalysis: Essays in Honor of Martin S. Bergmann.

Edited by Arlene Kramer Richards and Arnold D. Richards. Madison, CT: International Universities Press. 1994. Pp. 409.

Daniel Goldberg

Daniel Goldberg

Daniel A. Goldberg
1148 Fifth Avenue #1C, New York, NY 10128

Daniel Goldberg  

Since its beginnings with Montaigne in the sixteenth century, the essay has evolved from what Samuel Johnson called ‘an irregular or undigested piece’ into a form now defined (Webster's 3rd New International Dictionary) as ‘an analytic, interpretive or critical literary composition … usually dealing with a subject from a limited and often personal point of view’.

Typically using the essay, from multiple authors, as its compositional unit, the ‘festschrift’ requires intricate editorial balance. It is inherently a labour of love, the editors and contributors having a special personal or intellectual connection to the honoured subject. In this book, in honour of Martin S. Bergmann, an adoring preface, family pictures and a testimonial poem are followed by twenty-one essays, the authors often making clear their longstanding and devoted relationship to the man and his ideas. Indeed the tributes recalled—reminiscent of Bergmann's own film appearance—the question at the end of the film The Pelican Brief: might the absent, reclusive heroine be fictitious, as she ‘sounds too good to be true?’—and the answer ‘she almost is’.

More than encomium is required, however, for a commemorative collection to find a place in the minds and bookshelves of potential readers. The editorial burden is especially difficult when the essays cannot be bound under a single theme. In The

responseSet.title: The Unfinished Manuscript In The Drawer: Observations On The Analysis Of A Type Of Symptom
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Unfinished Manuscript In The Drawer: Observations On The Analysis Of A Type Of Symptom

Donald Kaplan

Donald Kaplan

Donald M. Kaplan
(Offprints from Louise J. Kaplan)
175 West 12th Street
New York
NY 10011

Donald Kaplan  

The interminable project, such as an unfinished manuscript in the drawer, is a familiar affliction in populations of writers, artists and intellectuals. An unrelenting absorption in such a project consuming whole decades of one's life distinguishes this plight from more benign varieties of work inhibition. The interminable project lends itself to a real dilemma of a double life. A case of a female writer and lecturer is presented, in which a longstanding unfinished manuscript figures as a principal complaint. In addition to familiar developmental conflicts, which psychoanalysis finds in psychopathologies of ambition and achievement, the analysis of this patient called for particular attention to various gains of the symptom itself; for example, its lending feasibiliy to the patient's alternative career by making of it something less ideal and therefore less crucial than it otherwise would have been. Moreover, the harrowing consumption of time that characterises such symptoms was also maintaining a state of moral masochism that gratified the patient's subdued ambivalence towards the analyst and the analysis. A literary project that exists principally to serve such ends as these expires as something feasible when the analysis has subtracted from it its extra-literary aims and devices.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Idea of the Past: History, Science and Practice in American Psychoanalysis.

By Leonard Jonathan Lamm. New York: New York University Press. 1993. Pp. 310 + xix.

Donald Spence

Donald Spence

Donald P. Spence
Rutgers Medical School, CMDNJ Department of Psychiatry
Box 101 University Heights, Piscataway, NJ 08854

Donald Spence  

We have come a long way from viewing the psychoanalytic process as a special brand of archeology, bent on uncovering ever-deeper layers of the patient's mind. It is now possible to believe that what is uncovered during a session is not necessarily a privileged account of before, a fragment of what really happened, but something quite different from the original. Indeed, Lamm tells us that ‘there may well be no robust relationship’ between what happens in the session and what happened in the past (p. 267). Moving to a more abstract level of argument, Lamm suggests that ‘there may well be a disjunction (within psychoanalytic discourse) between the theory of development and the theory of change’ (p. 267) and it would follow that treating a patient may have little in common with raising a child, despite the many current efforts to make this metaphor come true.

Rather than treat psychoanalysis as an excavation of memory which may lead us to the psychic bedrock of the analysand, Lamm wants us to learn how to create an empathic narrative which is ‘the verbal or linguistic medium through which the past is mastered and assimilated, if not exactly abolished and eliminated’ (p. 266). Narrative intervention, he reminds us, is a particular variety of discursive thinking which has close ties to the primary process and, as such, functions as a carrier for our experience and its residue of affective meanings (secondary process accounts tend to strip away the affect and emphasise the underlying logic). As retellings of the past begin to approach the patient's original experience, its various aspects (goes the theory) are worked through and laid to rest. The process of cure is thus directly linked to our skill in creating this empathic narrative which is clearly something more than reminding the patient of known facts about the past.

It can be seen that what might be called the analyst's theory of the past has a lot to do with how he constructs this linking narrative. Lamm makes a distinction between historical and scientific constructions of the past with the first limited to making meaning out of what happened, whereas the second tries to construct a model of development. (Discussion of these two approaches takes up more than half of the book.) The historical construction tends to be highly variable and is heavily influenced by the subjectivity of analyst and analysand while the scientific construction tends to be rooted in a specific clinical theory (Freudian, Kleinian, etc.).

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytical Therapy in the Hospital Setting.

By Paul Janssen. London and New York: Routledge. 1994. Pp. 204 + ix.

Paul Williams

Paul Williams

Paul Williams
1/15 Rosslyn Hill, London NW3 5UJ

Paul Williams  

This textbook reports on the cumulative researches of a hospital psychiatrist and psychoanalyst. Paul Janssen's thirty-year career has been concerned with the development of an optimal in-patient treatment setting for borderline and other disorders of early onset, grouped under the title ‘structural ego disorders’ (Furstenau, 1977)1 and which excludes overt psychosis. His work has taken place in Germany where he sought to integrate on one unit orthodox psychiatry, group psychotherapy, individual psychoanalytic psychotherapy and other representational media such as art, music and dance therapy. As such, this book will be of interest to psychiatrists and others working in psychiatric hospitals and therapeutic community-type settings. However, for any clinician interested in the transferential and social-structural implications of the use of group-analysis techniques alongside individual psychotherapy, and the potential effectiveness of this alliance, there are illuminating lessons to be learned from Professor Janssen's mistakes (freely admitted) and his successes.

Janssen's aim was ‘the design of an inpatient setting which truly harnesses and exploits all the elements present in a hospital environment’ (p. 3). He provides a summary of ways in which psychiatrists and therapeutic community pioneers (especially T. F. Main and the Cassel Hospital) have attempted to integrate inter-subjective activity with psychodynamic understanding, and acknowledges the influence of Main and Bion on the formation of his own unit. Balint and Winnicott figure prominently in his theoretical appraisal of borderline conditions. But Janssen is German, and he reveals (not least in a lengthy German bibliography) the extent to which German clinicians have addressed and resolved many of the problems associated with hospital treatment of severe mental illness.

1 Furstenau, P. (1977). Die beiden Dimensionen des psychoanalytischen Umgangs mit strukturell Ich-gestorten Patienten. Psyche, 31: 197-207.

[[RunningHead]]

Obituary: Adam Limentani 1913 - 1994

Mervin Glasser , Pearl King , Moses Laufer and Anne Hayman

Anne Hayman

Mervin Glasser
Pearl King
Moses Laufer
Anne Hayman
(Pearl King)
10 Lyndale Avenue
Child
’
s Hill
London NW2 2PY

Anne Hayman  

Adam Limentani, MD, Rome, died on Friday 9 September 1994 after a long illness. However, it was a sudden death, as he had recently recovered from an operation which had dealt satisfactorily with an ongoing problem. His wife Denise was told that he could return home after the weekend and though it was a terrible shock to her, his family and his friends, she felt that there was a consolation in that he did not have to live longer to face further suffering and that he died at a time when he was happy and free from pain. He had a distinguished career. President of the British Psycho-Analytical Society (1974-1977) and President of the IPA (1983-1986).

Adam came from a well established Roman non-orthodox Jewish family who eschewed ritual. He always affirmed his Jewish identity without being chauvinistic about it. From an early age he showed superior abilities. He was one of ten from three hundred applicants to become a student intern in the Rome Teaching Medical Clinic. However, this gain was short-lived because on 5 July 1938, after taking a special competitive examination to gain an appointment at the University Psychiatric Clinic, he emerged to learn from the newspapers that Italians had become an Aryan race: with that he lost the position of Clinical Assistant at the Medical Clinic as well as at the Neuro-Psychiatric Clinic (where he worked under Cerletti who introduced Electro-Convulsive Therapy!). Shortly after this he was stopped in the street by a policeman who asked him to show his papers in order to identify himself. ‘But’ he protested ‘You know me’, since they had been friendly at school. ‘That was yesterday’ replied the policeman.

The final event which indicated to him that he should leave Italy related to his having written his first thesis on medical hydrology in Italy in 1937. Having had a tutor, the rule was that the thesis would be published with one's tutor. The tutor came to Adam after the racial laws had been declared and asked him if he minded very much if they did not publish this paper together. Adam replied ‘By all means have it’. So, in December 1938, at the age of 25, he came to London.

[[RunningHead]]

Mahler and Kohut. Perspectives on Development, Psychopathology, and Technique.

Edited by Selma Kramer and Salman Akhtar. Northvale, New Jersey: Jason Aronson. 1994. Pp. 182 + xiv.

Steven Ablon

Steven Ablon

Steven L. Ablon
62 Chestnut Hill Road, Chestnut Hill
Massachusetts 02167

Steven Ablon  

The book, Mahler and Kohut, is a multi-authored volume that provides a concise and lucid description of the work of these two seminal psychoanalytic thinkers during the second part of this century. The authors discuss and compare recent developments and controversies in the areas of self psychology and separation-individuation theory. The strength of the book lies in its clear presentation of two important schools of thought that are compared in the light of contemporary thinking in psychoanalysis.

Selma Kramer’s introductory chapter, ‘The influence of Mahler and Kohut on psychoanalytic theory and practice’, provides an interesting personal and historical overview. Kramer acknowledges a partisanship with her mentor and friend, Margaret Mahler. From this perspective, Kramer presents well-known critiques of Kohut such as his underestimating the importance of constitutional characteristics and difficulties (‘as well as prenatal, postnatal, and neonatal experiences that may interfere with a child’s emotional ability to thrive, even with the best of mothering’, p. 8) and his lack of appreciation for the growth-promoting aspects of frustration. Furthermore, she criticises him for relying on material from reconstruction in analysis rather than prospective developmental research.

One of Kramer’s main points about Mahler is that she did not believe that material in analysis accurately represented experiences of the preverbal and early verbal periods. Kramer concludes that

Mahler’s contributions have a greater allegiance to the classical analytic theory than those of Kohut … Mahler did not intend to produce a compendium on psychoanalytic technique. Her great contributions center on development. Many of us have found these developmental findings very useful in our treatment of adults and children (pp. 13-14).

Despite her allegiance to Mahler, Kramer recognises that the usefulness of this book rests to a considerable extent on the dialogue between the perspectives of Kohut and Mahler producing ‘understandings that have hitherto eluded us’ (p. 14).
