By Bice Benvenuto. Cambridge: Polity Press. 1994. Pp. 169.

Giuseppina Antinucci-Mark

Giuseppina Antinucci-Mark

Giuseppina Antinucci-Mark
14 Montenotte Road, London N8 8RL

Giuseppina Antinucci-Mark  

Bice Benvenuto is an Italian Lacanian analyst who has been practising in London for many years. Her book is a comprehensive commentary on Lacan's concepts, based on seminars and lectures she has given over the years.

For this reader, the subtext that provides unity to this scholarly work is an exploration of the human mythmaking function that is the foundation of culture and is therefore the subject of enquiry at the point of intersection between psychoanalysis, anthropology, ethnology, linguistics and philosophy. This is particularly clear in Parts I and II, where she investigates the psychoanalytic process in relation to transference love, which is at the heart of it. Going back to the notion of malady of the soul, Benvenuto reminds us that psychoanalysis is the modern endeavour to heal the suffering soul. As such, psychoanalysis is a practice comparable to religion and philosophy. Their common element is the practice of love in its various forms: love of God, or love of knowledge, or transference love. Transference love is subsumed under the more general ‘discourse on love’, with its divisions into sacred and profane, sex and love, masculine and feminine, real and imaginary, conscious and unconscious.

This discourse on love structures and underlies the discussion of rites of passage and initiation rituals that ritualise the human encounter with love and eros. Benvenuto's discourse is expanded and transformed into a discourse on the drives within the Lacanian re-interpretation of drive theory. She puts forward the notion that the life and death instincts may be intimately fused. The death drive is somehow granted primacy when she claims that it operates through eros and is experienced by the subject as castration: ‘We could say that love intervenes imaginarily at the point when enjoyment must end: namely when castration intervenes’ (p. 13).

Adhering to Lacan's theory, she views castration as a marker of the symbolic entry into the ‘real’,

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Analytic Listening And The Analyst's Self-Analysis

Stephen Sonnenberg

Stephen Sonnenberg

Stephen M. Sonnenberg
900 East 30th Street
Suite 309
Austin
Texas 78705

Stephen Sonnenberg  

In recent decades analysts in North America have been writing about the challenge of listening to clinical material in ways which take account of the two person psychoanalytic situation. In that mutually regressive setting, self-analytic thinking on behalf of the analysand is essential for many analysts, because in it the analyst often relies on thoughts and feelings about conflicted and painful personal experience better to understand the analysand's inner experience. Effortful introspection allows some mastery, at least for the moment, of conflict which might otherwise prevent the analyst from thinking about and understanding what his inner experience may be telling him about his patient's mental life. In this essay the author describes the way an humiliating memory from his own childhood, recalled in response to his patient's dream, served as a cornerstone of his self-analytic effort on behalf of his patient. Coupled with self-analysis concerning his recent neck surgery, the analyst's self-reflections allowed him to be sensitive to a critical development in the analysis. This way of working complements the more traditional way analysts develop ideas from direct observation of the analysand in the consulting room.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Subject and Agency in Psychoanalysis—Which is to be Master?

By Frances M. Moran. New York and London: New York University Press. 1993. Pp. 198.

Raymond Cahn

Raymond Cahn

Raymond Cahn
6, rue de l
’
Abbaye, 75006 Paris

Raymond Cahn  

Frances M. Moran's project is a very ambitious one. Who speaks to the analyst, who is meant when the analyst refers to his patient's psychic functioning, unless it be the subject in the most concrete sense of the term? However, the polysemy of the word ‘subject’ gives rise to problems. Its semantic ‘spectrum’ extends between two extremes. The first and older of its meanings is the Greek hypokeimenon, the Latin subjectum, which denotes that which ‘remains lying beneath’, perhaps visible, perhaps concealed, but solid and permanent; while the second and more recent meaning relates to ‘what I am myself’, speaking, thinking, suffering, wishing and capable of describing itself in the self-referential mode. Another definition, closer to psychoanalysis, of a subject coming into being from the combination of all the agencies, and therefore equivalent to the personality as a whole, surely fails to do justice to the decisive dimension, the specificity of the unconscious.

Frances M. Moran's approach is different. Within this problem complex, she distinguishes the agency, which connotes the entity that determines or controls thoughts, words and actions, from the subject, a concept which she reserves for the individual who speaks to the analyst, thereby, in line with her theoretical orientation, placing it at the very centre of the clinical situation. It is from this standpoint that the author successively examines the work of Freud and the theories of Anna Freud, Hartmann, Melanie Klein and Lacan. Let us consider just a few examples.

In his correspondence with Fliess, Freud never asks himself who represses and why. He seems to be preoccupied mainly with the reasons why the repressed memories are of a sexual nature and with their fate. In The Interpretation of Dreams, not only does the location of the censorship (between Ucs. and Pcs. or between Pcs. and Cs.) remain doubtful, so that it is impossible to decide whether it belongs to the subject or the agency, but the origin of repression is attributed to the transformation of affect between the extremes of pleasure and unpleasure and hence to an energy-based causality in which the unconscious wishful instincts constitute ‘the core of our being’, while at the same time Freud refers to the subject which is divided yet likened to ‘an amalgamation of two separate people who are linked by some strong element in common’ (Freud, 1900, p. 581, footnote).

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Effects Of Trauma On Young Children: A Case Of 2-Year-Old Twins

Joyd. Osofsky , Geraldine Cohen and Martin Drell

Martin Drell

Joyd. Osofsky
Geraldine Cohen
Martin Drell
Department of Psychiatry
Louisiana State University Medical Center
1542 Tulane Avenue
New Orleans
LA 70112 2822

Martin Drell  

Trauma and its effects on early intrapsychic development are issues that need to be better understood as young children are exposed to ever increasing levels of violence. In this paper, we have considered trauma from the perspective of its effects on young children in the context of chronic community and family violence. Characteristic features of adults with post traumatic stress disorder have been identified in children under 4 years old who have been exposed to extreme trauma. In understanding trauma and its effects on children, it is important to consider how external events may impact on intrapsychic structure and the development of the self. A case is presented of identical 2 year, 11 month old twin boys who were referred after having witnessed the shooting death of their mother by their father seven months earlier. The course of psychoanalytically informed play psychotherapy is discussed with a major goal of the therapy being to help the children work through the trauma and return to a healthy developmental course. While the twins responded well to treatment and were able to work through traumatic material, systematic follow-up is needed to determine the later developmental course.

[[RunningHead]]

Freedom to Relate: Psychoanalytic Explorations.

By Roger Kennedy. London: Free Association Books. 1992. Pp. 200.

Chris Holland

Chris Holland

Chris Holland
16B Calton Hill, Edinburgh EH1 3BJ

Chris Holland  

‘The heart has its reasons’ is the theme of this book, which despite its modest form is very large in scope. Its ambitious intention is to build bridges of understanding between philosophy and psychoanalysis, and so it refers to an enormous range of writings in philosophy, while the essence of the psychoanalytical exploring and theorising is provided by case material.

Kennedy seeks to extend the dialogue between these disciplines, sensing that they tend to ignore or oversimplify each other's ideas through ignorance and fear. He hopes to encourage the hesitant to explore, so that each discipline may have more freedom to relate: the idea is that relating more freely to the other may give rise to a richer freedom within, as well as without, and so to more enjoyable debate and dialogue between psychoanalysis and philosophy. As the reader will be aware, this process is also the hope for the dialogue of the analytic couple; to develop new, more creative discourses.

Exploring the concept of freedom despite its endless misuse and misapplication, Kennedy, from the British Independent tradition, takes one particular form of it—‘freedom to relate’. He aims to ‘open up to the analysts areas of speculation that they too often ignore, and at the same time to challenge the notion of rationality that certain philosophers find too comfortable’. He is aware that he is unlikely to please either group, but intends that his base will be ‘wherever possible, the clinical encounter between analyst and analysand’ (p. 1).

The comprehensive introduction leads to nine psychoanalytical questions often avoided in analytical training, while the conclusions section is in the form of Kennedy's summarising answers. Learning always requires work, which is eased here by good writing replete with insights and information.

The ‘explorations in psychoanalysis’ of the subtitle occupy more than three whole chapters (chapters one and two, the first quarter of three, also chapter five), while the bulk of chapter three, and the whole of four—most illuminatingly—are almost entirely about philosophy, as is the final chapter (six). So it is 45 per cent analysis and 55 per cent philosophy; but 100 per cent mutual discourse in the transitional spaces of Kennedy's mind and book.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Desire in the Renaissance: Psychoanalysis and Literature.

Edited by Valeria Finucci and Regina Schwartz. Princeton: Princeton University Press. 1994. Pp. 270 + viii.

Peter Rudnytsky

Peter Rudnytsky

Peter L. Rudnytsky
Institute for the Psychological Study of the Arts
4008 Turlington Hall, University of Florida
Gainesville, FL 32611

Peter Rudnytsky  

Never before have I been asked to review the same book by more than one journal. Yet I received such an invitation for Desire in the Renaissance from not two but three different periodicals: in addition to the International Journal of Psycho-Analysis, the scholarly journals Renaissance Quarterly and Modern Philology contacted me about the book. If my experience is any indication, Desire in the Renaissance has clearly struck a nerve. This prompts one first to wonder what might make the book stand out from the welter of anthologies in the field of Renaissance literature that continue to pour forth from university presses and then to ask whether this attention is warranted.

The answer to the first question is not difficult to find; indeed, one has to look no further than the title. The conjunction between psychoanalysis (the preeminent intellectual movement of the twentieth century) and the Renaissance (the epoch that gave birth to modernity) detonates a powerful charge that is bound to be felt in many quarters. As Regina Schwartz & Valeria Finucci observe in their introduction, Freud in his elaboration of psychoanalytic theory turned regularly to artists and writers of the Renaissance: Leonardo da Vinci, Michelangelo, Ariosto (on the creative process), Tasso (on repetition), and of course Shakespeare—Hamlet, King Lear, The Merchant of Venice, and Macbeth being the plays that receive the most sustained attention—all figure in his work. But if ‘psychoanalysis is preoccupied with the literature of the Renaissance’, the editors argue, so ‘conversely, the writers of Renaissance literature were preoccupied with their version of the inner life, concerns that would come to constitute the purview of psychoanalysis’ (p. 3). Thus, extending an influential formulation by Shoshana Felman (1982), who has urged that the relationship of psychoanalysis to literature be conceived as one not of master-fully applied but rather of reciprocally implied knowledge, Schwartz & Finucci contend that the genres of Renaissance literature ‘are also spheres of knowledge about the psyche’ (p. 10), and thus interior rather than exterior to the domain of psychoanalysis.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

From Dependency to Autonomy: Studies in Organisation and Change.

By Eric Miller. London: Free Association Books. 1993. Pp. 342.

Alexander Pollock

Alexander Pollock

Alexander Pollock
14 Fitzjohn's Avenue, London NW3 5NA

Alexander Pollock  

This is an intriguing and stimulating book about problems of change and resistance in organisations, and the continuous development of a theory about the interplay between organisation, individuals, and groups on the one hand, and external consultants on the other. One of the several reasons it is of interest to psychoanalysts is that the theory includes, and in part develops from, psychoanalysis, especially its developments in the early work of Bion.

In his preface, Dr Miller introduces himself with a brief intellectual and professional autobiography. This is interesting and illuminating in its own right, and is also an explanation of how and why the book is compiled, for it is a compilation of papers that for the most part have been published previously. These were widely scattered, as the bibliography shows; together they form a coherent demonstration of the development of Miller's practice and theory. He is a long-standing member of the Tavistock Institute of Human Relations, and thus his book is also a demonstration of an important line of development of the Institute's work as it evolves through the professional life of an eminent member and his close colleagues.

Analysts will be particularly interested to see how analytic theory and practice (as understood in the UK) have been included in the conceptualisation of problems in a wide sampling of institutions. They will also see how it has been applied reflexively to the consultation process itself. All the work reported here is consultation, not survey or academic research, and this focus gives these papers their particular life and immediacy. It is not only this positioning and application of psychoanalysis which is of interest: we are all members of societies and institutes, and their sub-groupings, which have their own more or less difficult and often traumatic lives. Most analysts who read the book will have their own society in mind. The exercise is enlightening but not exhilarating; at one point Miller says, having considered one group of interventions: ‘Once again, looking back … I am reasonably satisfied with the analysis of the issues but depressed by the lack of any real change’. But this will prompt the idealistic reader to wonder whether the absence of any real change may not be a sign that the analysis has not after all been really satisfactory, or whether, given that the issue has been satisfactorily analysed, the problem itself has been adequately formulated in the first place. This is not an unfamiliar situation for analysts, and improving the analysis of a colleague's material is a familiar sport. The satisfactory formulation of a problem is something Miller returns to frequently, with the question of how to determine an ‘intelligible field of study’, and this is one of the subtextual themes running throughout the book.

responseSet.title: Art And Trauma
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Art And Trauma

Dori Laub and Daniel Podell

Daniel Podell

Dori Laub
Daniel Podell
(Dr Laub)
30 Ranch Road
Woodbridge, Connecticut CT 06525

Daniel Podell  

The authors of this paper attempt to show that ‘the art of trauma’, because of its indirect, unaestheticised and dialogic nature, may be the only possible medium for effective representations of trauma. The real witnessing presence created in the art of trauma can act as an antidote to the annihilation of the internal ‘other’ that occurs in the traumatic experience and to the resulting absence, which both constitutes the core of trauma and precludes its representation. Important elements of the art of trauma are illustrated using the work of Paul Celan, Anselm Kiefer, Claude Lanzmann, Art Spiegelman, and Anne-Marie Levine and texts by Aharon Appelfeld. Examining more closely what Holocaust survivors say in their testimonies, the authors contend that survival itself should be considered as a type of art of trauma when it is made possible by a creative comprehension of reality analogous to that which characterises more conventional forms of the art of trauma. The authors proceed to explore both the possible limits to the extent that trauma may be represented and the continuous struggle involved in attempting to ‘know’ trauma. They also discuss how art dealing with trauma may circumscribe a double locus: one of witnessing as well as one of emptiness or execution.

responseSet.title: Psychoanalysis in Transition: A Personal View.: By Merton M. Gill. Hillsdale, NJ: Analytic Press. 1994. Pp. 179 + xvii.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis in Transition: A Personal View.

By Merton M. Gill. Hillsdale, NJ: Analytic Press. 1994. Pp. 179 + xvii.

Sydney Pulver

Sydney Pulver

Sydney E. Pulver
111 North 49th Street, Philadelphia, PA 19139

Sydney Pulver  

I read this book in just a few sittings and could not refrain from writing an enthusiastic letter to Merton Gill. I had known him professionally for many years, and had always admired the clarity of his thought, his delight in intellectual debate and his genius in getting to the heart of the matter. I had been particularly impressed by his openness to new ideas and his ability to shift to new viewpoints when the evidence warranted it. This book, I felt, was his magnum opus, even more important than his work on transference. I could not wait for the review to come out to convey that to him. It was with shock and dismay that I received a note from Merton's wife thanking me for the letter and informing me that unfortunately he had died shortly before it arrived. This review, then, is written with a heavy heart. We have lost a brilliant psychoanalyst, a man whose writings helped shaped the thinking of a generation of psychoanalysts. Gill was always at the forefront of our thinking. He was eloquent, eminently clear, flexible, delightfully contentious, and always stimulating, even in his earlier expositions of some of the drier sides of ego psychology.

The volume is a fitting apogee to an illustrious career. Its style is unlike most of his other major works. Subtitled A Personal View, in style and content it is just that. Gill deals with those controversies in current psychoanalysis that he has taken an interest in, and this includes most that have had any real importance. The chapter headings illustrate this: ‘Constructivism and hermeneutics’; ‘The internal and the external’; ‘One-person and two-person psychology’; ‘Neutrality’; ‘Psychoanalysis and psychotherapy’; ‘Free association and the analytic process’; ‘What analysts say and do’; ‘Theory and technique’; and ‘The body in psychoanalysis’.

The book deserves more complete coverage than I can give it here. I shall briefly describe the contents of each chapter and say a little more about those aspects that were of particular interest to me. Unfortunately, one cannot convey its full flavour in this way, and readers may find themselves back in high school, trying to get a full appreciation of Hamlet by reading the Cliff Notes. But at least I will convey some of Gill's ideas, and I hope this will stimulate the reader to read the book itself.

responseSet.title: How to Survive as a Psychotherapist.: By Nina Coltart. London: Sheldon Press. 1993. Pp. 114.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

How to Survive as a Psychotherapist.

By Nina Coltart. London: Sheldon Press. 1993. Pp. 114.

Jean Arundale

Jean Arundale

Jean Arundale
11 Albert Place, London W8 5PD

Jean Arundale  

This book of essays, a few based on lectures to psychotherapy organisations but many newly-written pieces, both light-hearted and serious in its approach, has been collected as a guidebook to the profession. Nina Coltart is a well-known and distinguished Independent psychoanalyst, who was for ten years the Director of the London Clinic of Psycho-Analysis, and a past Vice-President of the British Psycho-Analytical Society. The author herself questions the choice of ‘survival’ as an apt metaphor for the practice of psychotherapy, suggestive as it is of teeth-gritting endurance of major catastrophe. Her transformation of the term from its usual meaning into one that describes positive enjoyment and creativity is a bit laboured, but Nina Coltart's own personality comes through as someone who, much more than simply enduring, has flourished and enjoyed her work and her life. With liveliness, humour and good sense, she has produced a fluent, candid trip through the career of the analyst/psychotherapist.

The problems of survival are loneliness, uncertainty, involvement in the suffering of others, etc., but Coltart reminds us that we take up this occupation by choice, so that, knowing what we are in for, we should have no cause for grumbling. In fact, her book might well serve as a warning beacon for those thinking of taking up the profession, pointing out the occupational hazards along the way. Perhaps forewarned is forearmed, but her presentation of training as a near-impossible obstacle course, might well put off the would-be trainee altogether. She makes her own advice to ‘sit back and enjoy’ the training seem rather unlikely. It helps, she says, to have a ‘vocation’ and a good analysis. After qualification, one's troubles have just begun, for to survive being thrown into the anxiety of independent work and expected to swim, while undergoing the bereavement of losing one's personal analysis and the special status of studentship gives the impression of having to recover from a sort of post-traumatic stress disorder. She does, of course, write of the benefits and rewards, too.

responseSet.title: Wilfred Bion: His Life and Work, 1897-1979.: By Gérard Bléandonu. Translated by Claire Pajaczkowska, with a foreword by R.D. Hinshelwood. New York: Guildford Press. 1994. Pp. 303 + xii.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Wilfred Bion: His Life and Work, 1897-1979.

By Gérard Bléandonu. Translated by Claire Pajaczkowska, with a foreword by R.D. Hinshelwood. New York: Guildford Press. 1994. Pp. 303 + xii.

Edna O'Shaughnessy

Edna O'Shaughnessy

Edna O'Shaughnessy
22 Heath Hurst Road, London NW3 3QA

Edna O'Shaughnessy  

In Gérard Bléandonu the life and the work of Wilfred Bion meet—most fortunately—a biographer and an expositor. Bléandonu is a Frenchman and a scholar, who, while cognisant of French traditions, has also had a longstanding regard for Klein (he published L'École de Melanie Klein in 1985), and the work of Bion. His aim in the book under review is ‘to reveal the meaning of given texts, in relation to the development, the historical context and the internal economy of Bion's oeuvre’.

He begins with biography: Bion's early childhood in India, his schooling in England, a plunge at 19 into the ordeal of the First World War from which he emerged with decorations, years of study—first at Oxford where he read History, captained the swimming team and obtained a rugger blue, then at Poitiers University, in order to study French language and literature, and, finally, after a spell as a teacher, University College, London, where he studied medicine. During his medical studies Bion went to a therapist (name unknown) whom he ironically dubbed ‘Mr Feel-it-in-the-past’. On qualifying Bion straightaway launched himself into psychiatry, joining the staff of the Tavistock in 1932, having further therapy from Hadfield. Later Bion approached Rickman, who analysed him between 1937 and 1939. The outbreak of the Second World War terminated this analysis prematurely, and brought patient and analyst together in a pioneering group project in the army. Such are the initial external events in Bion's life for Bléandonu the biographer.

Bléandonu never met Bion. His chief sources for the inner experience and the meaning of these years are the remarkable autobiographical writings of Bion's old age (published posthumously): The Long Weekend (1897-1919) Part of a Life, and All My Sins Remembered—Another Part of a Life—probably still in draft form—which ends with the Second World War and to which Francesca Bion added letters under the title The Other Side of Genius. Later in the book Bléandonu uses Bion's other writings, plus well-researched public sources, to bring to the reader a vivid vision of Bion the man. After all, biography must have a vision of its subject if it is not to remain a mere summation of the facts. Bléandonu is neither voyeuristic nor over-knowing—where he does not know the facts, he has proper authorial hesitation. And he judges his distance from his subject with sensitivity: psychologically aware, while remaining respectful of privacy. Bléandonu admires Bion enormously, especially his creativity, his witty and unusual turn of mind, as well as his courage, both psychological and physical; but he can see also pain, certain failings, and an adverse side of Bion's character. However, the account of the life is subsidiary to the achievements of Bion's work.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Babel of the Unconscious. Mother Tongue and Foreign Languages in the Psychoanalytic Dimension.

By Jacqueline Amati-Mehler, Simone Argentieri and Jorge Canestri. Madison, Connecticut: International Universities Press. 1993. Pp. 322.

Luis Rodriguez De La Sierra

Luis Rodriguez De La Sierra

Luis Rodriguez De La Sierra
15 Highwood House
 148 New Cavendish Street
London W1M 7FH

Luis Rodriguez De La Sierra  

I have always been interested in the subject of language, so I was immediately excited and enthusiastic to have the opportunity of both reading and reviewing this book. It has always surprised me that such an important theme has received so little attention in psychoanalytic circles, particularly in the British Society, where language and its vicissitudes are barely mentioned and whenever anyone dares to do so, finds little or no echo to their interest. This has always puzzled me, for the whole question of differences among languages, nuances, puns, jokes etc. contains a very rich, vast world of information about one's unconscious and also about one's external circumstances. This is not, of course, the case in other societies, where the importance of language has been taken into account more seriously, as in the case of the French analysts, who have been, to a lesser or greater degree, influenced by Jacques Lacan, who saw language as a very crucial issue in anyone's analysis. Perhaps the shadow of the majority of analysts’ inability to read Freud's original writings in German lurks heavily in the background. Bruno Bettelheim referred abundantly to this problem, which has given origin to some misconceptions due to the erroneous translation of certain words.

This is the review of the English translation of the original book reviewed by Adam Limentani in the Summer of 1992. Those who read it might experience a certain déjà vu feeling as inevitably we both say similar things on more than one occasion.

Copyright © Institute of Psycho-Analysis, London, 1995

responseSet.title: Autism and the Development of Mind.: By R. Peter Hobson. Hillsdale, NJ (USA) and Trowbridge (UK): Lawrence Erlbaum Associates. 1993. Pp. 246.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Autism and the Development of Mind.

By R. Peter Hobson. Hillsdale, NJ (USA) and Trowbridge (UK): Lawrence Erlbaum Associates. 1993. Pp. 246.

Robert Emde

Robert Emde

Robert M. Emde
Box C268-69, Univ. of Colorado, Medical Center
4200 East Ninth Avenue, Denver, CO 80262

Robert Emde  

Autism is an intriguing and troubling disorder. Its core symptoms include impairments in social interaction, communication, imagination, and repetitive stereotyped behaviours. Most autistic individuals are also intellectually impaired, although this impairment is not thought to be a core feature of the disorder. Compared with other childhood disorders, autism is uncommon—with a prevalence rate estimated to be about 5:10,000 for the full syndrome, and perhaps 21:10,000 for a ‘spectrum’ that would include milder forms of the disorder. Yet, in spite of its uncommonness, autism has attracted enormous research attention, with some of today's most brilliant and productive investigators trying to understand it. These include many working in psychology and psychiatry throughout the Englishspeaking world (which this book focuses on), with a significant proportion of these in the United Kingdom (where the author is Professor of Developmental Psychopathology at the Tavistock Clinic and at University College, London). Among such investigators, in addition to the author, are: Michael Rutter, Lorna Wing, Uta Firth, Alan Leslie, Simon Baron-Cohen, Dennis Cantwell, Marian Sigman, Donald Cohen, Peter Tanguay, Sally Rogers, Bruce Pennington, Geraldine Dawson, Deborah Fein, Theodore Shapiro, Helen Tager-Flusberg, Catherine Lord, and Peter Mundy—to name only a few of those whose work is reviewed in this book!

Why such attention? Perhaps it is because autism involves a basic disability in what is most human about us and what we usually take for granted, namely, our social connectedness. In Hobson's words, ‘one needs to convey what it is like to relate to an autistic individual, how it feels to try to communicate or otherwise become engaged with the child. In such a situation, it is not uncommon to feel that one is faced with a strangeling who moves on some other plane of existence, a person with whom one cannot “connect.” This experience of being with an autistic child seems to correspond with something essential that is lacking in the child's own experience of other people’ (p. 2).

responseSet.title: The Fragile Self: The Structure of Narcissistic Disturbance.: By Phil Mollon. London: Whurr Publishers. 1993. Pp. 206.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Fragile Self: The Structure of Narcissistic Disturbance.

By Phil Mollon. London: Whurr Publishers. 1993. Pp. 206.

John Keene

John Keene

John Keene
134 Marshalwick Lane, St Albans
Herts AL1 4XB

John Keene  

Mollon's aim is to establish the self as of central clinical and theoretical importance in psychoanalytic theory. His most original contribution is to organise the diverse phenomenological descriptions of disturbances of self experience into seven categories. This taxonomy is used in conjunction with themes extracted from the myth of Narcissus and Freud's writings on narcissism to evaluate nine theories of narcissistic disturbance, from which he constructs a composite model. The model is illustrated from the author's clinical work and Kohut's published case material and used as the basis for a review of the healing process.

The resulting model has two stages. The first emphasises how the achievements of a sense of efficacy and of the self as a centre of initiative depend on the infant's capacity to evoke appropriate and thoughtful responses from the mother. Mollon then argues that differentiation of self from other, the capacity for self-reflection, for facing the narcissistic blows inherent in recognising one's nonomnipotent position in the family and in the social and symbolic orders, all depend on the infant's capacity to tolerate its position in relation to the parents and their intercourse. The former development may be interfered with if the mother either fails to respond to the infant's communications, or her needs and phantasies of the infant dominate the interaction. The second stage may be compromised where there is no father, no intimacy for the child to experience exclusion from, or where the parents collude to exclude the father. The child then remains locked into a simultaneously gratifying and frightening dyad rather than experiencing the reliefs, pains and better reality testing of the third position. In addition to stressing the importance of the father in these processes, Mollon also points out the importance of shame, self-consciousness and narcissistic vulnerability in a range of psychiatric disorders and the beneficial effects when these are addressed in therapy.

responseSet.title: Femininities, Masculinities, Sexualities: Freud and Beyond.: By Nancy J. Chodorow. Lexington: University Press of Kentucky and London: Free Association Books. 1994. Pp. 132.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Femininities, Masculinities, Sexualities: Freud and Beyond.

By Nancy J. Chodorow. Lexington: University Press of Kentucky and London: Free Association Books. 1994. Pp. 132.

Ethel Person

Ethel Person

Ethel Spector Person
135 Central Park West, New York NY 10023

Ethel Person  

In this latest, closely reasoned book Chodorow casts a cool eye on two assumptions that continue to inform—and deform—psychoanalytic theories of sex and gender. The first of these assumptions gives rise to ‘universalizing gender theories’. As Chodorow points out, feminist, gay and lesbian theorists have raised objections to the essentialist assumption that there are but ‘two normative modes of development—that of the girl and that of the boy’—and the biological determinism that underlies this assumption. These same critics have also challenged the second assumption, which has to do with the ‘normative status of heterosexuality’. This heterosexual bias gives rise, they say, to the ‘devaluation of women, homophobia, and heterosexism found in much psychoanalytic writing … ’

Chodorow’s book is best understood in the context of the history of our field and of sexology. While no branch of science is value-free, nowhere is this more the case than in fields like sexology, which are invariably saturated with the prevailing prejudices of the cultural milieu. The scientific study of sex first surged only in the 1870s, when masturbation, homosexuality and deviance were discussed from a medical point of view (Sulloway, 1979). The putative evils of masturbation (depletion and insanity) were culturally deplored and clinically documented to the satisfaction of most of the medical profession (Marcus, 1964). Yet little attention was paid to ‘normal’ sexuality, other than to the biology of reproduction.

Around the turn of the century, however, the narrow interest in pathological sexuality and sexual physiology gradually gave way to a broader interest in ‘normal’ sexuality. Originating in part from the general psychiatric interest in concepts of normality, the study of normal sexuality also had its roots, ironically enough, in the contemporary fascination with deviance. The study of such perversions as pain-dependence and sexual sadism, for example, necessarily opened scientists’ eyes to the fact that the domain of sexuality extended beyond the genitals. Thus it was that the scientific imagination enlarged so as to conceive of the distinction between sexuality and procreation, and the idea of erotogenic zones other than the genitals entered the literature.

responseSet.title: A Silenced Indian: More On The Secret Fantasy Of Being An Exception
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Silenced Indian: More On The Secret Fantasy Of Being An Exception

Antonie Ladan

Antonie Ladan

Antonie Ladan
Pauw v Wieldrechtlaan 21
3701 et Zeist
The Netherlands

Antonie Ladan  

By means of some aspects of an analysis, the author shows how the fear of being murdered may form part of the secret fantasy of being an exception. This fantasy should offer protection against being helplessly at the mercy of intense feelings which cannot be integrated, and of an intolerable loss of the sense of self, which renders true separation impossible. During the course of development the fantasy gradually assumes its definitive form. One element of this definitive form is that the analysand exists in a state of adulthood as he imagined it as a child. Control and autonomy here seem guaranteed, and dependence, helplessness and ridiculousness have been overcome. For the creation of such a fantasy, the analysand makes ‘a great leap forward’ at one or more stages of his development. The author indicates how a leap of this kind could be imagined in intrapsychic terms.

responseSet.title: Between Author and Reader: A Psychoanalytic Approach to Writing and Reading.: By Stanley J. Coen. New York: Columbia University Press. 1994. Pp. 210.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Between Author and Reader: A Psychoanalytic Approach to Writing and Reading.

By Stanley J. Coen. New York: Columbia University Press. 1994. Pp. 210.

Robert Paul

Robert Paul

Robert A. Paul
1925 Clairmont Road, Suite 130, Decatur, GA 30033

Robert Paul  

Coen’s thoughtful and valuable book is a response, within the changing climate of current psychoanalytic practice, to the question of what (if anything) psychoanalysis can bring to the study of literature. Coen argues that the frequent focus of psychoanalytic criticism on the identification of psychosexual fantasy content in a work of literature, understood as a compromise formation, leads to premature explanatory closure of a kind we would not find satisfactory in contemporary clinical practice. Too often, Coen suggests, attempts to analyse authors on the basis of their works, or to treat fictional characters as if they themselves could be analysed, yield reductionistic or unwarranted conclusions that are insensitive to the actual responses in the here and now of the reader experiencing the open-ended complexities of the work. Just as analytic practice has moved away from simply informing patients of what unconscious fantasy is at work as they freely associate, and towards focusing instead on what is actually happening between the two people in the room, so too in the realm of literature we should examine the interaction between an author and a reader, asking the same sort of questions we ask ourselves in practising analysis: what response does one person in this situation wish to elicit from the other at this moment, and why, and how?

Coen provides models of his suggested procedure by examining the cases of four writers: Jean Gênet, Louis-Ferdinand Céline, Marquis de Sade, and Freud in his letters to Fliess. The argument, briefly encapsulated, is that writers, like patients, experience feelings of isolation, loneliness, neglect, and depression, and require readers, just as patients require analysts, to bring them back to life. But this is a problem because often effective writers experience themselves as rageful, destructive and needy, and are fearful that the other from who they hope to elicit vivifying connection will be repelled and will fail to give them the narcissistic gratification and admiration they need. Authors therefore adopt a strategy of externalising their most loathsome inner characteristics, transforming them through artistic craft into things of beauty, and then demanding that the reader love and admire them. In this theory Coen’s interest in the perversions is manifest, for the relation between writer and reader, like that between the relatively more ‘primitive’ patient and the analyst, is motivated by many of the same dynamics that characterise a perverse sexual relationship, dominated as it is by seduction, control, and sadism. Readers are, in effect, asked to hold still and witness, contain, and survive the writer’s act of giving to them the writer’s enviousness and destructiveness in the form of the artistic work itself.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Essential Papers on Literature and Psychoanalysis.

Edited by Emanuel Berman. New York: New York University Press. 1993. Pp. 516.

Helen Robinson

Helen Robinson

Helen Taylor Robinson
4 Whellock Road, London W4 1DZ

Helen Robinson  

As with dreams, Freud discovered the inordinate usefulness of a fixed, immutable text in literature to demonstrate the expertise of his newly-invented, singular creation, psychoanalysis. It was expedient to show his first psychoanalytic formulations to the public, as creator and author in his own right (not the joint author of Studies on Hysteria, 1893-5, which were ‘studies’, ‘essays’, we might say ‘work-in-progress’, without the fuller confidence of the work on dreams) in the analysis and interpretation of dreams.

For dreams, like literature, were, and are, symbolic artefacts with a clinical basis. Someone dreams, and someone fictionalises, so that a human figure, dreamer or artist, can be claimed from behind the artefact, and, by means of this obvious link, the validation of the psychoanalytic process and the demonstration of its methodology can be attempted. How the dream and the dreamer, and the literary creation and its creator, connect was to become, through Freud, the stuff of history, and the continuing outpouring of psychoanalytic writings, good, bad and indifferent, has followed from it.

This fixed immutability of text, of terra firma (dream, slip of the tongue, joke, and thence, with increased confidence, play, poem, poetic or dramatic character, thence artistic character, or, indeed, virtually any art-form)

responseSet.title: Feminism and Psychoanalysis; A Critical Dictionary.: Edited by Elizabeth Wright et al. Oxford: Blackwell. 1992. Pp. 485.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Feminism and Psychoanalysis; A Critical Dictionary.

Edited by Elizabeth Wright et al. Oxford: Blackwell. 1992. Pp. 485.

Susan Lipshitz-Phillips

Susan Lipshitz-Phillips

Susan Lipshitz-Phillips
34 Rotherwick Road, London NW11

Susan Lipshitz-Phillips  

This dictionary represents a milestone in the annexation of psychoanalysis by academics and feminists in Europe and America. For those students reading this book now, the days before Lacan was translated and crossed the Channel and the Atlantic are presumably the Dark Ages. It must be difficult for them to imagine the near-hysterical reactions to the first theory of patriarchy put to early feminist conferences some twenty years ago.

The outrage expressed then at the loss of plain English and at the radical challenge to classical empiricist psychology and extant radical politics is reminiscent of the reception given to psychoanalysis in the early days. Freud' s experience of his audiences' disbelief when confronted with his theory of infantile sexuality demonstrated a powerful collective resistance to the idea of the unconscious, and in this case it is arguably a similar demand for recognition of the unconscious that made the theory unpalatable. However, some feminists began to pursue the study of psychoanalytic psychology as it became clear that the theories of sexual politics could not ignore this unconscious dimension in the way that classical Marxist writings had done.

At first glance the tone and topics of this book might suggest that the shock impact of psychoanalytic and Lacanian theories has waned and that they have become culturally acceptable. So although the book generally presupposes a basic grounding in these ideas, it is well written and clear enough to be accessible to the non-specialist reader for whom feminism is a modern fact. However, there are still problems within the stated project of this dictionary, when psychoanalytic and feminist ideas are put together and common concepts are discussed.

The authors use three dimensions to approach their entries; the historical, the psychological and A to Z, covering for example, androgyny, desire, essentialism, female patients, Kristeva, Klein, literature, sadomasochism, voyeurism and the Womens' Therapy Centre. With such a range of material taken at different levels, the book seems a bit uneven, so it probably works best if read as a series of summary seminars. The contributions are written almost exclusively by academics and women and pose questions of theory that may well and perhaps should interest clinicians.

[[RunningHead]]

Freud, Race, and Gender.

By Sander L. Gilman. Princeton, New Jersey: Princeton University Press. 1993. Pp. 277 + xvi.

John Kafka

John Kafka

John S. Kafka
5323 Connecticutt Avenue N.W., #802
Washington DC 20015

John Kafka  

Studies of anti-Semitism occupy a prominent place among the publications of Sander L. Gilman, a cultural historian and a historian of medicine. In a previous book, Gilman (1986) presented Freud as a case study of Jewish self-hatred and developed the thesis that this ambivalence about his Jewishness influenced many aspects of his life and work. The striking new thesis developed in Freud, Race, and Gender is that Freud's attitudes towards femininity are expressions of his displaced ambivalence towards Jewishness.

In the introduction to Freud, Race, and Gender Gilman reviews the literature and discusses previous treatments of Freud's Jewish identity. He then sketches his original perspective and outlines the plan for his book.

Copyright © Institute of Psycho-Analysis, London, 1995

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Frances Tustin.

By Sheila Spensley. London and New York: Routledge. 1995. Pp. 154.

Kate Barrows

Kate Barrows

Kate Barrows
16 Sydenham Lane, Cotham, Bristol BS6 5SQ

Kate Barrows  

This is the first book to be published on the work of Frances Tustin, who died in November 1994. Her innovative ideas about autism are widely recognised as having made a central contribution to psychoanalytic work with autistic children and to the understanding of autistic features in adults. Trained as a child psychotherapist at the Tavistock Clinic, she was later made an Honorary Affiliate of the British Psycho-Analytical Society in recognition of the significance of her work.

Spensley’s book is published in the Makers of Modern Psychotherapy series, aimed at making the work and ideas of the ‘makers’ accessible to the non-specialist. Psychotherapists and psychoanalysts will also be interested in this book, as it attempts to link Tustin’s ideas to other psychoanalytic theories about the earliest relationship between physical and emotional life.

A description of Tustin’s childhood and early professional development is followed by an account of the history of approaches to autism and childhood psychosis. Throughout the book, concepts central to Tustin’s thinking are discussed and linked to the contributions of psychoanalysts, particularly of Ogden and Grotstein, as well as to the work of developmental psychologists. Spensley also includes other areas that particularly interest her: the application of Tustin’s ideas to work with mental handicap and learning difficulties, and to religion and myth as containers for primitive anxieties.

Spensley’s work and her thinking owe a great deal to Tustin; she cites numerous clinical examples that illustrate Tustin’s ideas in a way that may speak to the non-specialist. Her examples from work with adult patients, including adults classified as mentally disabled, and from children with learning difficulties, demonstrate the prevalence of autistic ways of functioning. Where behaviour might be seen as, at best, incomprehensible, and at worst wilfully aggressive, Spensley shows how an understanding of the primitive anxieties that autistic manoeuvres keep at bay can lead to a more realistic acceptance of the individual’s plight. This can also lead to more appropriate decisions about treatment and management of cases.

responseSet.title: On Trying To Teach: The Mind in Correspondence.: By M. Robert Gardner. Hillsdale, NJ: Analytic Press. 1994. Pp. 163.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Trying To Teach: The Mind in Correspondence.

By M. Robert Gardner. Hillsdale, NJ: Analytic Press. 1994. Pp. 163.

James McLaughlin

James McLaughlin

James T. McLaughlin
820 Devonshire Street, Pittsburgh, PA 15213

James McLaughlin  

M. Robert Gardner has followed his earlier book Self Inquiry—an enquiry into trying to be an analyst—with an analytic scanning of the broader dilemma of how to try to be a teacher to someone.

This slim work deserves to be read by all of us who, as parent, friend, lover, companion, and especially teacher, have been moved by the divine curse to teach something to someone, and then have had to face the consequences. Much of what he has to say, in ways both witty and profound, goes to the heart of our enduring analytic dilemma over the tension between exploring the unknown and providing enlightenment.

With abiding compassion and respect for teachers and pupils, Gardner recounts his dilemmas and challenges in his own struggles towards a way and philosophy of teaching. He does so with short, freestanding essays, which in their aggregate he has chosen aptly to designate a palimpsest.

In Book I: The School of Soft Knocks, Gardner etches his personal perspective on the history of teaching. At first gently and playfully, he teases out the empty spots and contradictions in those teachers, artists and poets of past and present whom he likes to refer to as true teachers. All share the true teacher's furore to teach, lethal when present, deadening when not.

The author has funny, poignant and multiple views about everything he touches, particularly when he is lampooning the dogmatic, the self-assured and pontificating propensities in us teachers.

A few words are in order about his style. He combines the leisurely pace of an essayist with the verbal gifts of the shenachie, the story-teller, repeating words and phrases with relish and emphasis, reversing and undoing their original meaning and metaphor at every turn. These sudden jolts and twistings are deliberate and artful.

Copyright © Institute of Psycho-Analysis, London, 1995

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Crisis at Adolescence. Object Relations Theory with the Family.

Edited by Sally Box et al. Northvale, NJ: Jason Aronson. 1994. Pp. 275.

Luigi Caparrotta

Luigi Caparrotta

Luigi Caparrotta
17 St Gabriels Road, London NW2 4DS

Luigi Caparrotta  

This book is an attractive collection of papers based on work with families and adolescents. The preface and initial résumé make no bones about the copyright of what the authors call ‘the Tavistock-Klein object-relations approach’, a term which appears throughout the book.

The main theoretical underpinning is in fact based mainly on the psychoanalytic views of Klein and Bion and some of their followers. This emphasises the understanding of the level of shared fantasies and unconscious assumptions of the ‘here and now’, both in the individual and in group dynamics as applied to the family setting. It also illustrates how the use of the therapists’ counter-transference experience is pivotal to the often chaotic interaction occurring in disturbed families as portrayed in the various clinical examples.

The book is divided into three main sections comprising three, four and seven chapters respectively.

Copyright © Institute of Psycho-Analysis, London, 1995

1074

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psyche and the Social World: Developments in Group-Analytic Theory.

Edited by Dennis Brown and Louis Zinkin. London/New York: Routledge. 1994. Pp. 280 + xii.

D. Abse

D. Abse

D. Wilfred Abse
1023 Millmont Street, Charlottesville, VA 22901

D. Abse  

Though this book is a group production embodying the results of considerable group discussion and much consensus, based on a sharing of S. H. Foulkes's pioneering psychoanalytic orientation to group therapy, the authors also separately develop different emphases and some points of divergence from one another. Moreover, in attempting to extend the theory of therapeutic group analysis by means of incorporating variant systems approaches and some shaky anthropological and philosophical viewpoints, some of these authors occasionally overwhelm pertinent consideration of the application to group therapy of clinically-anchored basic concepts of Freud's psychoanalysis, as, for example, those summarised so well in Otto Fenichel's The Psychoanalytic Theory of Neurosis (1945).

There is an interesting introductory chapter by the editors, Dennis Brown & Louis Zinkin, linking their work with Foulkes's legacy, and they also provide a final chapter, which constitutes a valiant attempt to bring together some of the discordances. Their overview, explicitly not intended as a critical review, certainly does not pre-empt one.

responseSet.title: The Autonomous Self: The Work of John D. Sutherland.: Edited by Jill Savege Scharff. Northvale, New Jersey/London: Jason Aronson. 1994. Pp. xxv + 454.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Autonomous Self: The Work of John D. Sutherland.

Edited by Jill Savege Scharff. Northvale, New Jersey/London: Jason Aronson. 1994. Pp. xxv + 454.

John Padel

John Padel

John Padel
16 Staverton Road, Oxford OX2 6XJ

John Padel  

Jock Sutherland died in 1991 at the age of 86, and it is an unexpected pleasure to welcome a book of his collected writings and lectures—unexpected because he did so many other things in his life that it was difficult to realise that he had written so much. Like only two other psychoanalysts, John Bowlby and Anna Freud, Sutherland was awarded the CBE—for his work for over twenty years as Medical Director of the Tavistock Clinic. Besides having been the Editor of the International Journal of Psycho-Analysis and of the British Journal of Medical Psychology, he was also the Editor of the International Library of Psycho-Analysis and saw twenty-eight books through to publication. He was not only a training analyst in the British Society and co-founder of the Scottish Institute of Human Relations, but he also became a consultant to institutions in Topeka, Washington and New York, where he regularly visited and taught from the mid-1960s onwards. In the Epilogue, Dr Scharff says that Sutherland ‘had a profound impact on psychiatry in the United States’ and ‘persuaded American psychoanalysis to stretch its ego-psychology orientation to include the tenets of British objectrelations theory’ (p. 425), introducing Fairbairn's work, then little known, to American psychiatry.

The book has been beautifully edited by Dr Scharff. To make three of the twenty-five chapters, she has collated or combined material from different sources without any trace of seams or patching. The last two chapters are transcriptions from audiocassettes of a talk at the Washington School of Psychiatry and of informal teaching interviews in Washington and New York. Four of the chapters were papers Sutherland co-authored: with H. S. Gill (two); Gill and H. Phillipson; and I. E. Menzies.

Confronted with the prospect of reading through so many papers from various sources, I turned first to the last chapter, ‘Reminiscences’, and was rewarded with a vivid memory of Sutherland talking and teaching informally. There are questions and answers, and Drs Gordon and Corrigan (New York) manage to elicit statements about Sutherland's early professional history and also his spontaneous opinions and recollections of Fairbairn and Harry Guntrip. Dr David Scharff (Washington) draws him to talk first about Fairbairn's personal psychopathology and then through the experience of writing his book, Fairbairn's Journey into the Interior (in six months at the age of 83!), to the effects which the research and the writing had had on him. In answering, Sutherland reveals the humility, and even the lack of self-assurance (especially about his creative ability and his capacity to synthesise his perceptions), which underlay all his achievements. He links these achievements with a degree of ‘false self’ derived from his mother's ambitions for him, while he ‘relegated father to the garden’; in writing the book he felt he had proved to himself the truth that creative activity is the outcome of the harmonious union of both parents internally (he had signalled the re-institution of his father by starting to wear a kilt!). Other questioners, especially Dr Jill Scharff, get him to express more of his thoughts on the self and about Fairbairn's theories of defence.

responseSet.title: Personal Relations Therapy: The Collected Papers of H. J. S. Guntrip.: Edited by Jeremy Hazell. Northvale NJ: Jason Aronson. 1994. Pp 430.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Personal Relations Therapy: The Collected Papers of H. J. S. Guntrip.

Edited by Jeremy Hazell. Northvale NJ: Jason Aronson. 1994. Pp 430.

Eric Rayner

Eric Rayner

Eric Rayner
43 Heath Hurst Road, London NW3 2RU

Eric Rayner  

It is twenty years since Harry Guntrip died. His most creative time was in the nineteen fifties and sixties. He has, I think, a definite place in psychoanalytic history as Fairbairn's most vigorous and readable exponent. In his own right he can be counted as one of the founders of systematic object-relations thinking. His ideas about the nature of schizoid structures have had a lasting impact. His style, beautifully illustrated by a host of clinical vignettes, is pungently lucid. Quite a few professionals have mentioned that it was reading Guntrip that brought them to psychoanalysis. This is all the more remarkable as Guntrip was a nonconformist minister who worked in Leeds far from any centre of psychoanalytic thought. Despite not having had any formal therapeutic training he had an average of twelve patients a day for many years.

It is Guntrip's books that are best known; but this complete collection of papers will be useful not only to the historical scholar but also to the general reader, who will be able to get a clear introduction to his ideas by just dipping into some of the chapters here.

Guntrip's early papers developed Fairbairn's fundamental reformation of the classical instinct model into a thoroughgoing psychoanalytic object-relations theory. Next comes the main fruit Guntrip obtains from this vision—the exposition of schizoid mechanisms. This is followed by papers where Winnicott takes over from Fairbairn as Guntrip's main influence; here we have a centring upon therapeutic technique, especially upon the fear of and the need for regression in the therapy. Then, coming from Guntrip's last years, is a miscellany of essays of a philosophical rather than a technical nature. How does he appear now thirty and forty years later as we come towards the end of the century?

I remember hearing Guntrip speak thirty years ago and my reaction to him was quite complex. Frankly, there was something unlikeable about him; this was a note of a sermonising preacher in his tone. It still appears at times in his writing; his simple clarity of thought can turn into crude over-simplified certainties. He can then too readily dismiss ideas that do not suit him. For instance, there is an unreflective spurning of the centrality given in classical theory to the Oedipus complex; this treatment will be annoying to any thoughtful psychoanalytic reader. Likewise, there is a rather coarse brushing aside of all mathematical thinking as merely schizoid and pathological. Thus Bertrand Russell's historic Principia Mathematica is thrown on to the conceptual scrap-heap without a second thought. In fact, when Guntrip gets into a philosophising frame of mind he is usually at his worst. He does not appear a truly philosophical thinker. There is also something unpleasantly self-absorbed about his descriptions of his early home life and of his analyses with Fairbairn and Winnicott.

responseSet.title: The Curve of Life: Correspondence of Heinz Kohut: 1923-1981. Edited by Geoffrey Cocks. Chicago: The University of Chicago Press. 1994. Pp. 443 (including 15 illustrations).
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Curve of Life: Correspondence of Heinz Kohut

1923-1981. Edited by Geoffrey Cocks. Chicago: The University of Chicago Press. 1994. Pp. 443 (including 15 illustrations).

Ernest Wolf

Ernest Wolf

Ernest Wolf
1333 Tower Road, Winnetka, Illinois 60093

Ernest Wolf  

Kohut was one of the most influential psychoanalysts of recent decades. Even those colleagues who disagreed with his ideas and with his modifications of more traditional psychoanalytic thought will grant his ability to sway co-workers and students inside and outside of psychoanalysis to give serious consideration to his psychology of the self. As the writer of three books and more than a dozen scientific papers he became a centre of attention and controversy in the psychoanalytic community. The private correspondence of such a creative psychoanalytic theoretician and clinician is of great interest for the elaboration and clarification it adds to the published oeuvre. Ninety-three letters from the last twenty years of Kohut's life have already appeared in The Search for the Self: Selected Writings of Heinz Kohut. In the present volume, Geoffrey Cocks, a historian, makes public an additional three hundred plus letters that have never been published before. They range from a note to his Daddy by 7-year-old Heinz to a brief letter to his son Tom a few weeks before Kohut's death in 1981. The first letters, all but one sent from Vienna before Kohut's emigration in March 1939, were addressed to his closest friend Siegmund Levarie in Chicago. After the war the rest of the letters emanated from his Chicago home or from travel or a holiday sojourn.

The editor's thirty-one-page introduction presents a valuable biographical-historical background as well as acquainting the reader with some of the issues that informed Kohut's work and his relationships with his psychoanalytic colleagues. In spite of Kohut's long and close friendships with a circle of collaborators and with others, he remained essentially a very private person who did not share much of his inner life nor the details of his personal past history. To the natural guardedness of the lonely boy grown into a celebrated adult must be added the ever present concern of the psychoanalyst for confidentiality and for preserving for himself a certain anonymity vis-à-vis past, present and potential future analysands. Therefore, we owe Geoffrey Cocks recognition for publishing in his introduction the first extensive biographical sketch of Kohut.

responseSet.title: The Yiddish Theatre, The Oklahoma Theatre And Kafka's Salvation
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Yiddish Theatre, The Oklahoma Theatre And Kafka's Salvation

William Jeffrey

William Jeffrey

William D. Jeffrey
29 Eighth Avenue
Brooklyn
NY 11217

William Jeffrey  

The author discusses Kafka's enthralment with the Yiddish theatre, which performed in Prague in the autumn and winter of 1911. Examination of Kafka's ‘Diaries’ suggests that an important factor in this response was his infatuation with two actresses. They evoked fantasies of avoiding oedipal conflict by surrendering adult male sexuality and its threat of castration by regressing to a prephallic relationship with a maternal woman who would provide a nurturing relationship. Later, when writing the final chapter of his novel ‘Amerika’ in 1914, he creatively expressed a derivation of this fantasy when the protagonist, Karl Rossman, achieved a sort of salvation by joining the Oklahoma theatre. In the final year of his life Kafka was able to leave home to live with a woman, and in this achievement he fulfilled aspects of this fantasy.

responseSet.title: In The Wake Of Heinz Hartmann
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

In The Wake Of Heinz Hartmann

Roy Schafer

Roy Schafer

Roy Schafer
241 Central Park West, Ste. 1A
New York
NY 10024

Roy Schafer  

This study of Heinz Hartmann's contributions emphasises change within continuity. Specifically, it is argued that he both integrated and changed significantly Freud's structural theory—his ‘ego psychology’. This he did through his then-modern concept of adaptation. In turn, adaptation paved the way towards further change in the direction of current emphases on dialogue and intersubjectivity. These new developments have contributed beneficially to theory and clinical practice, but, like all new developments, they have introduced new problems, among them unreflective and undisciplined eclecticism. Consideration is given to the incompleteness inherent in theory construction. In psychoanalysis, the new, object-related developments require as a complement some version of the type of structural theorising initiated by Freud and developed to its highest point by Hartmann and his close collaborators particularly; in meeting that requirement, some version of an objectivist stance is also required to complement the subjectivism and pluralism of the newer developments. Throughout this appreciation of Hartmann's role in the history of psychoanalytic thought, an effort is made to undo the negative effects of misreadings that took his ideas on adaptation and ego psychology to mean an endorsement of superficial adjustment or conformity and an overvaluation of conscious mental processes.

<autaff><addr><ln><cs lang="en" style="AuthorName">Herbert J. Schlesinger</cs></ln><ln>65 Fifth Avenue</ln><ln>New York</ln><ln>New York 10003</ln></addr></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: Facts Is Facts—Or Is They?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Facts Is Facts—Or Is They?

Herbert Schlesinger

Herbert Schlesinger

Herbert J. Schlesinger
65 Fifth Avenue
New York
New York 10003

Herbert Schlesinger  

The author argues that to consider the place of facts in psychoanalysis one must first of all distinguish between the use of the term fact in its everyday sense, in which its opposite would be untruth, from the scientific sense in which the term refers to a construction that has been awarded the status of fact by the theory in which it is embedded. Theories in sciences of human behaviour or experience must include not just the hierarchical arrangement of concepts and constructs of the physical sciences, but also the process orientation in which time is the major organising dimension. Facts, as constructs, have different significance in each of these versions of theory, and the meaning of validation, as well as the procedures for determining validity, also differ.

responseSet.title: The Diffident Narcissist: A Character-Type Illustrated In The Beast In The Jungle By Henry James
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Diffident Narcissist: A Character-Type Illustrated In The Beast In The Jungle By Henry James

Winslow Hunt

Winslow Hunt

Winslow Hunt
640 Pershing
Pocatello
Idaho 83201

Winslow Hunt  

The author demonstrates that Henry James's novella ‘The Beast in the Jungle’ can be read in the light of a psychoanalytic understanding of narcissism. Although James does not describe the hero's childhood, it can be imagined from the perspective of analytic understanding. The character painted by James is characterised by a persona of conventionality, decorum and inhibited sexuality and assertiveness. His inner life is dominated by a secret grandiose fantasy. This combination leads the author to call this type the diffident narcissist. The type bears a relationship to the covert narcissist described by Akhtar, Cooper and others in various contributions. James traces his hero's development from young adulthood to old age, through his evolving relationship with a kind and empathic woman. Because he waits for a transforming event that never happens, this man fails to seize the love and happiness that real life has offered him.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychodynamic Treatment Research: A Handbook for Clinical Practice.

Edited by Nancy E. Miller, Lester Luborsky, Jacques P. Barber and John P. Docherty. New York: Basic Books. 1993. Pp. 577.

Roger MacKinnon and Susan Vaughan

Susan Vaughan

Roger A. MacKinnon
Susan C. Vaughan
11 E. 87th Street, New York, NY 10028

Susan Vaughan  

This well-written, thorough book is an important contribution to the literature of psychotherapy and a valuable review of current psychodynamic psychotherapy research. In 26 chapters, leading psychotherapy researchers summarise the impressive gains made in investigating psychodynamic treatments. Clinical and research findings are integrated to address topics such as the importance of the therapeutic alliance in predicting treatment outcome and the construction of measures to assess transference. The emphasis on the quantitative investigation of key clinical concepts is a major strength of the book. This volume will appeal to psychotherapy researchers and those in training who are interested in the current state of psychodynamic research. Teachers may find it useful in that it describes how central concepts are defined and measured and explores the relationship of these concepts to treatment outcome.

The Ego and Analysis of Defense.

By Paul Gray. Northvale, NJ: Jason Aronson. 1994. Pp. 254 + xxvi.

Steven Levy

Steven Levy

Steven Levy
Psychoanalytic Institute, Department of Psychiatry
Emory University School of Medicine
1701 Uppergate Drive, Rooom 414
Atlanta, GA 303422

Steven Levy  

In Paul Gray's The Ego and Analysis of Defense, we have a sequential compilation of the author's contributions to the psychoanalytic literature, centring on psychoanalytic technique and its theoretical underpinnings. The central aim of Gray's clinical approach concerns maximising the analysand's capacity for self-observation, especially in relation to the activation of defence in the face of intrapsychic conflict. Gray's work has been enormously influential in crystallising issues relating to the technical management of defence analysis, a therapeutic activity taken for granted, but in Gray's view, requiring more careful scrutiny than is usually accorded it in discussions of technique.

Analysts have been slow to operationalise technically Freud's structural model described in The Ego and the Id and Symptoms, Inhibitions and Anxiety.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Ethical And Unethical Conduct In Psychoanalysis. Correlations Between Logic, Ethics And Science

Gregorio Klimovsky , Susana Dupetit and Samuel Zysman

Samuel Zysman

Gregorio Klimovsky
Susana Dupetit
Samuel Zysman
Virrey Loreto 2446, PB
1426 Buenos Aires

Samuel Zysman  

This paper, written by a logician and two psychoanalysts, deals with the existence of logical relationships between ethics and psychoanalysis, which can be expressed as propositions. With a view to focusing the discussion on this point, the authors have deliberately abstained from any polemics concerning the scientific status of psychoanalysis, and concentrate on a consideration of the ethical implications of the conduct of psychoanalysts in the exercise of their profession. In this approach, the theoretical statements and technical rules of psychoanalysis as manifested in its daily practice inevitably have connexions with metaethics and normative ethics. The authors discuss in turn the ethical classification of human conduct, the classes of ethical propositions, the problem of means, ends and values, and the concrete ethical questions raised by the practice of psychoanalysis. Their argument is based on consideration of the existence of empirical, pure ethical and mixed ethical statements, analogous to the familiar categories of empirical, theoretical and mixed statements, or rules of correspondence. Mixed ethical statements are deemed to serve as new rules of correspondence, whereby logical bridges could be constructed between the factual aspect of psychoanalysis and pure ethical statements. If this approach is valid, a sufficient foundation exists to express views on the ethical characteristics of psychoanalytic conduct.

This book is a collection of essays by authors from a diversity of professional disciplines and backgrounds. The editors are a psychoanalyst/sociologist and a sociologist/social anthropologist. The other authors include an anthropologist, a philosopher with an interest in ethics, a general practitioner, a historian of medicine, an osteopath and naturopath, a professor of sociology, a lawyer who specialises in medical issues, and a gynaecologist. The papers vary considerably in quality, and in the breadth and depth of their approach to the theme of the book.

responseSet.title: Analytic Listening And The Experience Of Surprise
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Analytic Listening And The Experience Of Surprise

Henry Smith

Henry Smith

Henry F. Smith
17 Hammond Street
Cambridge, Massachusetts 02138-1915

Henry Smith  

The analyst's experience of surprise, its relation to different models of analytic listening and its function in the analytic process is illustrated and explored through a series of clinical vignettes. Surprise is a crucial affective ingredient of the analyst's attention and data-gathering. It is multiply determined and inevitably reflects some discovery or rediscovery on the part of the analyst about both the patient and the analyst. In the dynamic tension of the analyst's listening there is always an interplay between expectation and surprise, as each new facet of the patient's conflictual organisation is revealed. Evenly-hovering attention entails the setting aside of conscious expectation and so maximises the potential for curiosity, surprise and discovery. The author describes the relation of surprise to transient identifications with the patient and to the interventions that result, the relation of surprise to the sense of the uncanny, and the shifts in the analyst's defensive organisation that allow for the experience of surprise. Several vignettes illustrate the interplay of the analyst's and the patient's psychologies and the manner in which surprise may alert the analyst to mutually-created resistances and enactments, which may appear to be discrete phenomena, but are in fact continuous processes, intrinsic to the work.

responseSet.title: ‘Et In Arcadia Ego … ?’
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

‘Et In Arcadia Ego … ?’

Riccardo Steiner

Riccardo Steiner

Riccardo Steiner
12A Belsize Lane
London NW3

Riccardo Steiner  

The author uses archival material, mainly the letters exchanged between Jones and Strachey in the fifties, when Jones was writing his biography of Freud and Strachey was working on the Standard Edition. He shows the complexity of the interaction between these two men; the more or less conscious ambivalences, motivations and resentments against the so-called Freudian establishment of that time. This per se can help us to understand how a ‘certain image’ of Freud was created in Great Britain during those years and the reasons for its authoritative persistence. Yet the paper touches on more general issues such as those of the fascination with psychoanalytic archives and the more or less accepted and admitted unconscious motivations of the interpreters of these documents too, not excluding myself. The conclusion reached is that even psychoanalytical documents do not rest in a peaceful Arcadia and that archives cannot be considered as the Arcadia of documents—at least not in the ways that Arcadia has always been considered and interpreted, misleadingly, even in two paintings by Poussin, which I refer to in my paper. These in reality remind us that in Arcadia there is death too. Even the memory that the documents should help us to conserve is ineluctably bound to loss, dissemination, mutilation and uncertainty, and therefore must also face the presence of death also.

responseSet.title: Analysing Forms Of Aliveness And Deadness Of The Transference-Countertransference
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Analysing Forms Of Aliveness And Deadness Of The Transference-Countertransference

Thomas Ogden

Thomas Ogden

Thomas H. Ogden
306 Laurel Street
San Francisco
CA 94118

Thomas Ogden  

The sense of aliveness and deadness of the transference-countertransference represents a critical dimension of the analytic experience and may be the single most important measure of the moment-to-moment status of the analytic process. In this paper the author presents four clinical discussions that illustrate the importance of analysing the experience of aliveness and deadness in (of) the transference-countertransference. In each vignette particular emphasis is placed on the use of transference interpretation derived from experience in the countertransference to address the defensive and expressive role of the dynamic movement of aliveness and deadness at a given juncture in an analysis. The role played by the experience of aliveness and deadness in the structure of the patient's internal object world and quality of object relatedness is examined.
